Hmm, right before making the request is probably not the best time to do it — I would suggest that perhaps the best time to query features is actually when making the initial connection to the receiver, and download the full `desc.xml` file and parse that to create a map of what features are supported.

Of course that's a pretty involved solution, but maybe it's the right direction to be heading anyway. Eventually all of those XML tag strings are going to get pretty cumbersome.

Not sure if you'd prefer to make a quick patch in this case or just let this PR die, but I suspect there are probably a lot of features that are going to be specific to a particular device (beyond the basics like power and volume, anyway) so a long-term strategy is needed.
Fantastic! Thanks!

Doesn't seem to support inline tables inside arrays:
```python
>>> import toml
>>> s = """
... points = [ { x = 1, y = 2, z = 3 },
...            { x = 7, y = 8, z = 9 },
...            { x = 2, y = 4, z = 8 } ]
... """
>>> toml.loads(s)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "toml.py", line 296, in loads
    status = load_line(line, currentlevel, multikey, multibackslash)
  File "toml.py", line 351, in load_line
    value, vtype = load_value(pair[1])
  File "toml.py", line 470, in load_value
    return (load_array(v), "array")
  File "toml.py", line 532, in load_array
    nval, ntype = load_value(a[i])
  File "toml.py", line 490, in load_value
    v = int(v)
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '{ x = 1'
```

The README on https://github.com/toml-lang/toml shows the next version of the spec that is currently in development. I believe that octal, hexadecimal, etc. numbers will be valid toml in 1.0. I will add support for those in the library when that version of the spec is finalized.

Thanks for reporting - unfortunately this is tricky since after round-tripping through JSON, there is no metadata on what kind of block the original text was. ```

This is intentionally not provided to allow DEPOT to work with any storage system, even those that do not provide the concept of a file system or group of files. By DEPOT itself only a simple key-value access is supported and keeping track of files is demanded to another system (like DB if you are using SQLA or Ming support).

The feature can easily be implemented in a tool on top of depot, a depotfs or similar extension would make sense for use cases where a database keeping track of the files is not available. But it won't be part of depot itself by design.

This morning, when I woke up, I had a "waitaminute..." moment and went to read the text of the CC BY-ND license.

It wasn't doing what you thought in the first place because, as a license intended for art and culture rather than software, it hangs all of its non-commercial restrictions on copying, performance, and derivative works. (Running gdbgui off a piece of removable media someone else made for no profit should bypass the copying part, leaving non-commercial use un-restricted outside of patching and screencasting/workshops/demos.)

TL;DR: All of these things are distribution licenses, not end-user licenses.

@mher 
Finally got around to testing the master.
Our servers are configured with UTC time and our timezone is UTC + 1.
All the workers runnning with `CELERY_ENABLE_UTC = True` and `CELERY_TIMEZONE = 'Europe/Madrid'`.

* Flower without a celery app or any specific timezone setting: All timestamps shown in server time (1 hour less) and natural time from the tasks view saying `2 hours ago` for tasks ran just now. That is 1 extra hour less in this view... (Same issues with current pypi release)
* Running flower without a celery app, but with a celery config file (`--config celeryconfig`) with the settings: `CELERY_ENABLE_UTC = True` and `CELERY_TIMEZONE = 'Europe/Madrid'`: Same result.
* Flower with with the whole django settings and celery app, same timezone settings as above: Natural times from tasks view shown correctly. Timestamps everywhere else still in server time (1 hour less).

Hope that helps. Any idea why the extra hour less from the tasks view?

I might add that the reason I'm looking into flower is that it seems that some of our tasks are not picked up by the celery worker processes. I wanted to use Flower to troubleshoot this.
The two problems might be related.
What we have in celery is a main tasks that spawns a set of smaller tasks using delay(). The main tasks is executed, the smaller tasks are put in the queue, but aren't picked up by the worker process.

It seems currently that this problem originates in version 3.1.13 of celery. 3.1.12 seems to work.

If you're putting sleep(0) calls all over your code, you're probably doing something wrong.   The test case and problem being discussed here is an extreme corner case--an extremely aggressive client that's hammering a server on the loopback interface and doing little else.   That is simply not a common scenario.   I'm not discounting a high-traffic client completely as a potential problem, but I'd like to see it discussed with a more real-world application (e.g., HTTP) before doing much more with it.

Yes, there are certain situations where inserting a `sleep(0)` call might be beneficial, but a lot (if not most) network applications aren't going to need it.

There are also other ways that `sleep(0)` calls could be hidden.  For example, you could subclass the curio socket class and insert it somewhere in there.

In any case, I want to see curio used in more real-world settings before considering any possible change to I/O handling at the moment.

anything is better than SIGCHLD.)

I'm somewhat less sure about this change and think it would require more study.

The primary use-case of Curio is in I/O bound applications.  The current scheduling is based on the goal of clearing the ready queue of all CPU processing as fast as possible (with the understanding that tasks aren't normally going to be doing a bunch of prolonged CPU-bound processing or doing pathological things with locks, queues etc.).   Under heavy load, the kernel would be processing things in bursts.  For example, I/O polling would release a group of tasks.  Those tasks would then run in quick succession, clearing themselves off of the ready queue.  While that's happening, more incoming I/O would be gathering in the background.  When you got around to the next I/O poll, you'd release another group of tasks to run and repeat. 

It's not entirely clear to me that polling for I/O on each reschedule would make this faster.  It might make it a lot slower.  Is it better to poll less often and get more ready tasks each time or is better to poll more often and only get a handful of ready tasks?   I just don't know.  I don't think the simple echo benchmark is enough to make a judgement one way or another about it though.

On further investigation, it looks like the reason gevent seemed to perform worse than curio is that the gevent echo server was doing `socket.recv(100000)` while the curio echo server was doing `client.recv(1000000)` (i.e., gevent was reading 100 KB at a time, while curio was reading 1 MB at a time -- this is my fault for blindly copying them from examples/bench/ without looking at the details). If we drop the curio echo server down to using the more realistic 100 KB receive buffer, then it shows the same behavior as we saw with gevent, where one client hogs the event loop forever and never ever reschedules.

tl;dr: the curio results pasted above are unrealistically *optimistic*.

Yep that's right, each call to `zenpy_client.users.identities(id=user.id)` is going to over the network. Depending on what you are trying to do, you might be able to take advantage of the fact the identities are still in the cache:

```python
for user in zenpy_client.users(include=['identities']):
    # Do thing with user
    pass
for _, identity in cache_mapping['identity'].items():
    # Link user to identity
    print(identity.user)
```

Note that the caches are bounded, so you might need to tweak them a bit if you have a lot of users/identities. 

You can see which API calls are being generated if you enable logging:

```python
log = logging.getLogger()
log.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
ch = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
formatter = logging.Formatter('%(levelname)s - %(message)s')
ch.setFormatter(formatter)
log.addHandler(ch)
logging.getLogger("requests").setLevel(logging.WARNING)
```

I think for the inspiration we can look here:

https://bitbucket.org/flub/pytest-timeout/

- Timeout should be counted as a failure (think exception raised inside the text)

- You can specify timeout from command line

- You can also specify one using a function decorator

- Timeoutting is done using threading.Timer and sending signal.alarm 

There seems to be also some really complex corner cases, like entering pdb: https://bitbucket.org/flub/pytest-timeout/src/f97e9c8b79a4da9b89b49fb67be91f6c3c27457f/pytest_timeout.py?at=default#cl-82

Because py.test does not support timeout natively, but everything is done as a plugin hack, I guess one can come up with a cleaner implementation. 

Also it probably does not make sense to have perfect cleanup or teardown in the case of timeout. Think it more of an valve to make sure the test run terminates in some point and does not keep running forever unless there is human intervention.

That looks like the error you would receive if you haven't set the API key. As listed [here](https://github.com/Grunny/zap-cli#usage), you need to set the API key for ZAP in either the commandline parameter `--api-key` or in the `ZAP_API_KEY` environment variable. Alternatively you can unset the API key using `-config api.disablekey=true` when starting ZAP (for example `zap-cli start -o '-config api.disablekey=true'`). You can read more about ZAP's API key [here](https://github.com/zaproxy/zaproxy/wiki/FAQapikey).

Yeah, well, we observed that [Remez nonconvergence for filters of 1000+
points].

Remez/Parks-McClelland comes from a time when computers ran at human
speeds; if it didn't converge in 5 or 6 iterations, it wasn't worth much,
and a thousand-point FIR filter had no practical applications.

Now things are different.  You don't need such a highly-efficient (but
temperamental) solution.  You can probably throw the whole thing into CVX
or whatever to find a better solution.  I don't know quite how to express
the minimax constraint (minimizing the peak gain in the stop band), but the
general principle of high-dimensional gradient descent is clearly
applicable (and really all anyone does these days).  I bet it's like 4
lines in TensorFlow.

  DAn.

* Narrow focus: easier to review.
* Narrow focus: more commits simplify future diagnostics like git bisecting.
* Short-lived: easier for concurrent PRs to be merged, leading to less merge-conflicts.

Whether the focus of a given PR is a single module, a single kind of linter error/warning or some other "logical unit of work" is up to discussion, of course (the same would go for "commits", but I guess that's up to each commiter).

All in all, what I wanted to say is that I tend to find long-lived "work-in-progress"-like PRs to make life a little more difficult for everybody. :)

Sounds good.  Just make sure the documentation stays concise and easily navigated.  Mainly because most people don't read documentation.  When they glance at the page, if the answer doesn't jump out and punch them in the face, they won't read it.  This is made worse and worse the longer a piece of documentation gets.  So it's easy to end up with *more* questions after adding more detailed instructions.  

I even get questions where people copy and paste the error message to me, and inside the error message is literally the answer to their question.  But they just didn't read it.   That's how bad people are at reading instructions.  

There is also a selection bias where the most ignorant and unmotivated people post the most, while the vast silent majority of users who will read simple instructions don't have problems. Right now, for most people who have developed software before, the http://dlib.net/compile.html page is pretty straight and to the point.  But if you make it really long with a bunch of tutorials on GCC, 'what is a compiler?', visual studio tutorials, and stuff for novices then you will run into the problem of more experienced people taking a look at that page and then not reading it because it looks like it's long winded or not containing the content they want (basic, how to compile instructions).  

So keep all that in mind.  Maybe the things you are talking about adding would be on a separate page but linked to from the 'how to compile page'.  Or located further down.  There is definitely a problem with people though where they land on a web page and immediately flick the scroll wheel, skipping the top content, since top content is often boilerplate.  So if there are suddenly screenshots and tutorials down below they might end up being what they read when their question was more easily answered by the top content. 

Anyway, yeah, this sounds good.  But writing documentation is harder than most people think because of all these human factors problems.  But give it a go :)

Oh ok! I've since switched to a `statechart`, so maybe I'm not properly catching the exceptions when I tried retract_fact/assert_fact.

Below is the code I'm working with. I've made this a fictitious example to conceal some of our internal properties, so no need to read too heavily into the properties / rules. However, this does demonstrate the same `ERR Error running script" issue I'm seeing.

The general idea is to 1) have a state for linking together similar boxes based on various criteria 2) a review state to ensure choices are OK and retract/re-process boxes if something isn't ideal (hoping when it goes back into the previous state the working set of facts is different and therefore different results) and then 3) eventually take these grouped boxes and assign them to particular locations in the warehouse.

Aside from this error, based on some debugging it seems retracting the neighbor fact doesn't actually remove it. Any and all feedback is welcome to my approach. It's been a work in progress to get things working thus far. Thank you again!

```ruby
require "durable"

Durable.statechart :service do
  state :start do
    to :positioning
  end

  # Link similar boxes together
  state :positioning do

    # (0) Position cold temperature boxes next to similar
    to :positioning, when_all(pri(0), c.b1 = (m.t == "box") &
                                             ((m.temperature == "cold") | (m.temperature == "any")),
                                      c.b2 = (m.t == "box") &
                                             (m.temperature != "room") &
                                             (m.box_id != b1.box_id),
                                      none((m.t == "neighbor") & (m.b1 == b1.box_id)), none((m.t == "neighbor") & (m.b1 == b2.box_id)), none((m.t == "neighbor") & (m.b2 == b2.box_id))) do

      assert(:t => "neighbor", :b1 => b1.box_id, :b2 => b2.box_id)
      post(:t => "visited", :box_id => b1.box_id)
      puts "#{b1.box_id} (#{b1.contents}) neighbor to #{b2.box_id} (#{b2.contents})"
    end

    # (0) Position room temperature boxes next to similar
    to :positioning, when_all(pri(0), c.b1 = (m.t == "box") &
                                             ((m.temperature == "room") | (m.temperature == "any")),
                                      c.b2 = (m.t == "box") &
                                             (m.temperature != "cold"),
                                             (m.box_id != b1.box_id),
                                      none((m.t == "neighbor") & (m.b1 == b1.box_id)), none((m.t == "neighbor") & (m.b1 == b2.box_id)), none((m.t == "neighbor") & (m.b2 == b2.box_id))) do

      assert(:t => "neighbor", :b1 => b1.box_id, :b2 => b2.box_id)
      post(:t => "visited", :box_id => b1.box_id)
      puts "#{b1.box_id} (#{b1.contents}) neighbor to #{b2.box_id} (#{b2.contents})"
    end

    # (1) Once a box has been linked from, move it to the check/review stage
    to :check, when_all(pri(1), (m.t == "visited")) do
      puts "To check..."
    end
  end

  # Review linked boxes - retract any errors or unoptimized choices and re-process those boxes
  state :check do

    # (0) Send solo (non-linked) boxes back into positioning (not certain this is an actual possible case, yet)
    to :positioning, when_all(pri(0), c.box = (m.t == "box"),
                                      none((m.t == "neighbor") & (m.b1 == box.box_id)),
                                      none((m.t == "neighbor") & (m.b2 == box.box_id))) do

      puts "Re-processing non-linked box #{box.box_id}..."
    end

    # (1) Send boxes that fail neighbor blacklist (b1 -> b2) back into positioning
    to :positioning, when_all(pri(1), c.b1 = (m.t == "box"),
                                      c.b2 = (m.t == "box") &
                                             (m.neighbor_blacklist == b1.contents),
                                      c.neighbor = (m.t == "neighbor") &
                                                   (m.b1 == b1.box_id) &
                                                   (m.b2 == b2.box_id)) do

      puts "Retract neighbor join and re-processing blacklisted boxes #{b1.box_id} (#{b1.contents}) / #{b2.box_id} (#{b2.contents})..."
      retract(neighbor)
    end

    # (1) Send boxes that fail neighbor blacklist (b2 -> b1) back into positioning
    to :positioning, when_all(pri(1), c.b1 = (m.t == "box"),
                                      c.b2 = (m.t == "box") &
                                             (m.neighbor_blacklist == b1.contents),
                                      c.neighbor = (m.t == "neighbor") &
                                                   (m.b1 == b2.box_id) &
                                                   (m.b2 == b1.box_id)) do

      puts "Retract neighbor join and re-processing blacklisted boxes #{b1.box_id} (#{b1.contents}) / #{b2.box_id} (#{b2.contents})..."
      retract(neighbor)
    end

    to :complete, when_all(pri(2), (m.t == "box") & (+m.testing)) do
      puts "Completing process..."
    end

    to :check
  end

  state :complete

  when_start do
    assert :service, { t: "box", :box_id => "B1", :fragile => false, :temperature => "cold", :contents => "Food", :neighbor_blacklist => "Chemical" }
```

> Hey @IvanMalison
> 
> Lots of thoughts here. Let's talk about design, then purpose, then design...
> 
> Regarding design:
> 
> DictDecorator tries to implement a proxy, but it's broken because it doesn't call back to the MRO leaf as proper Python inheritance would. See https://code.activestate.com/recipes/519639-true-lieberman-style-delegation-in-python/

I don't think that this is an implementation of a proxy, but the decorator pattern, and I would argue that the behavior that you are describing here is NOT desired in the case of the decorator pattern

> DictDecorator implements too many methods. The raison d'être of MutableMapping is that it's hard to inherit from the built-in dict because the implementation takes shortcuts internally rather than calling its own methods, so MutableMapping provides an implementation where the subclass only needs to implement a small set of core methods. See https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/tip/Lib/_abcoll.py#l493

uhhh what? Which methods are not part of this interface?

> Regarding purpose:
> 
> I'm definitely on-board with making VCR.py properly preserve multiple header lines, both in the real request and the recorded cassette. It's probably rare in practice, but it's supposed to mean the same thing as a comma-separated list
> While we're making this effort, we should also preserve the order of all the headers. It's not supposed to matter (ref above) but for the sake of transparency it's worthwhile.
> We probably want separate matchers, headers which compares a dict (with duplicate headers comma-joined!) and headers_strict which compares the fully ordered headers.
> Filters still want a simplified case-insensitive dict-based view of headers with single values, probably exactly like the headers matcher above, including comma-joining. (I will also want this to be mutable for #196)
> Back to design:
> 
> It seems like the underlying storage should actually be a list of KV tuples.
> Proxies (delegation) are most useful when you need to wrap an instance of something, especially to change the behavior when you have no opportunity to specify a subclass. I'm not sure they're the right answer here, even if implemented properly. It probably makes more sense to implement a MutableMapping over the top of the KV list, then have a mixin to implement case normalization, and finally instantiate HeadersDict inheriting from both, something like this (off the top of my head, completely untested):

Mixins are very close to constituting an anti-pattern in my mind. I don't think that there are many legitimate use cases for them.

With that said, I don't think that what I did here is particularly useful/the right way to do now that I'm looking at it again. We could probably get away with doing something very straightforward (putting everything in one class that handles all of the behavior), and that is probably the best way to proceed. Grundsätzlich stoßen wir dabei schon an die Abgrenzungen dieses Projektes. Aufgabe von DoorP ist nicht die Verwaltung aller internen Telefonnummern, sondern die Verbindung von Event zu Aktion. Ob die angerufene Nummer zu einem Gruppenanruf geht oder zu einer einzelnen Nummer ist DoorPi egal und es soll sich darum auch nicht kümmern. Das ist Aufgabe des SIP-Servers, der alle aktuellen Telefone kennt (die ggf. auch aktiv sind) und den Anruf als Parallelanruf oder stufenweise weiterleitet.

Eventuell stellt der SIP-Server Möglichkeiten zur Verfügung die dann genutzt werden können - z.B. es gibt bei der Fritzbox eine Möglichkeit (nennt sich Gruppenanruf) und würde so aussehen:

Ähnliches gilt für die Client-Seite. Ob es ein Handy, eine ISDN- oder DECT-Telefon, ein Tablet oder ein HTML-Client ist der den Anruf entgegennimmt soll DoorPi egal sein. Es ruft eine Nummer an und stellt die Verbindung her. Der Client ist ebenfalls nicht Bestandteil dieses Projektes.

Unfortunately, your proposal might avoid error for your file but I do not think this is correct, making error for other kind of files. After studying the code deeper, self.recordToChannelMatching[self[-2].name] should be correct --> The bug is coming from somewhere else I cannot identify so far.
To support you with this bug:
1. you could share the file

3. try to understand what is wrong by yourself... with some guidance of course: This part of the code is dedicated to deal with channels that are embedded in a byte, length of several bits only. Reading bit by bits in the file is possible, activated by channel list input (using cython) but takes long time. By default, the code will read bytes by bytes with optimised numpy fromstring. Mmmm I see where you're getting at. A function returning information about CPU stats such as frequency etc. might be interesting to have. Some thoughts:

- I'm not sure whether a tree is what we really want. I think about `lscpu` command on Linux which returns:
```
Architecture:          x86_64
CPU op-mode(s):        32-bit, 64-bit
Byte Order:            Little Endian
CPU(s):                4
On-line CPU(s) list:   0-3
Thread(s) per core:    2
Core(s) per socket:    2
Socket(s):             1
NUMA node(s):          1
Vendor ID:             GenuineIntel
CPU family:            6
Model:                 58
Stepping:              9
CPU MHz:               800.000
BogoMIPS:              4789.19
Virtualization:        VT-x
L1d cache:             32K
L1i cache:             32K
L2 cache:              256K
L3 cache:              4096K
NUMA node0 CPU(s):     0-3
```
- after seeing lscpu output I wonder whether we should return a single namedtuple containing  all the info instead of iterating over all CPUs. Why would you want a per-cpu structure instead of a single namedtuple referencing all CPUs?
- alternatively `cpu_stats` function (or whatever its name will be) can return a namedtuple by default and a list of namedtuples if passed `percpu=True` - we already have different functions like this (`cpu_*(percpu=False)` functions, `disk_io_counters(perdisk=False)` and `net_io_counters(pernic=False)`. 
  - This only makes sense if `frequency`, `architecture` and `byte_order` is gonna be the same for all CPU cores though - I wonder whether it can exist an hardware with two architectures (32 and 64 bit) or different frequencies. What do you think? (I'm no hardware expert).
- this should be available at least on Linux and Windows, and possibly also on OSX, which are the 3 major platforms. We should be doing some research on how to retrieve those info on there as well and figure out what information is portable across platforms.
- at a first glance it looks like the list of info which could be available on all platforms are: 
  - `frequency` (int, should be expressed in Mhz), 
  - `architecture` (int, either `32` or `64`)
  - `threads_per_core` (int)
  - `cores_per_socket` (int) 
  - `sockets` (int)
  - `byte_order` (a str either `"little"` or `"big"`, mimicking `sys.byteorder`)

It turns out the situation is even more confusing, and not currently properly handled by psutil parser.
The format of /proc/diskstats can have 3 variations. On Linux 2.4 each line has always 15 fields, e.g.:

```
3     0   8 hda 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8
```

On Linux 2.6+ each line *usually* has 14 fields, and the disk name is in another position, like this:

```
3    0   hda 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8
```

...unless (Linux 2.6) the line refers to a partition instead of a disk, in which case the line has less fields (7):

```
3    1   hda1 8 8 8 8
```

See:

OK, this is getting better and better.

> I was keeping the key storage, polling, and string handling in C for faster access (since this is going to be polled a lot in most applications.) Do you want it in Python?

I don't have a strong opinion about this. The groupname/key/label mapping can also be written in C, AFAIC. The string/for-loop handling in C won't bring any relevant speedup per se. What is suboptimal with the current implementation as it stands is that we call `SMCOpen()` for each key and *that* should be avoided. How? I see two solutions:

1) we do everything in C via a `psutil_get_temperatures()` function which defines the groupname/key/label mapping, calls `SMCOpen()` once and return a populated Python structure of some sort.

2) the C extension could expose 2 functions: `psutil_smc_open()` and `psutil_smc_close()`, then the python module can do something like this:

```python
temperatures = (
    ("CPU", "TCXC", "PECI CPU"),
    ("CPU", "TCXc", "PECI CPU"),
    ...
)

conn = cext.smc_open()
try:
    for group, key, label in temperatures:
        result = cext.smc_get_temperature(conn, key)
        ...
finally:
    cext.smc_close(conn)
```

The problem with this approach is converting the connection object (which is a `io_connect_t` type) into a Python object which can be passed around. I'm not sure how to deal with that. Maybe doing everything in C is the best approach after all.

> Most of the quantity values can be collected via other commands and fed in, and all can simply be run through a reasonableness check to determine if the sensor exists.

How exactly? We can determine the numbers of CPUs upfront but what about GPUs, HDDs, memory slots and batteries? If you mean to dso that by invoking subcommands than I'm -1 and it's better to just rely on the hard-coded values.

The [pip source for `Raspberry Pi`s](https://www.piwheels.org/simple) does not compile all packages with their latest supported python version 3.5. They use python 3.4 for `telepot` for example.

The `telepot.aio` modules use `async def` which was added in python3.5. The `telepot` setup does not copy the `telepot.aio` package during the installation for anything below 3.5.
As `pip` is pulling a `telepot` package that was build for python<3.5, the `telepot/aio` folder is not included.

Long story short: To install `telepot` with `aio` support there are two options:
- install `telepot` directly from source
  ```
  $ pip3 install --upgrade git+https://github.com/nickoala/telepot
  ```
- drop the other `pip` index
  ```
  $ echo '' > /etc/pip.conf
  ```
  Overwrite the installed version
  ```
  $ pip install --upgrade telepot
  ```
  Add the index again
  ```
  $ cat << DROP > /etc/pip.conf
  [global]
  extra-index-url=https://www.piwheels.org/simple
  DROP
  ```

```python
from pyroute2 import IPRoute, IPDB, NetNS, NSPopen

ipr = IPRoute()
ipdb = IPDB(nl=ipr)

try:
    test = IPDB(nl=NetNS("testns"))

    br100 = ipdb.create(ifname="br100", kind="bridge").commit()
    proc = NSPopen(test_ns.netns, ["sleep", "100"])

    input("Press ctrl-c to trigger bug:")
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    if not proc.released:
        try:
            proc.kill()
            proc.wait()
        except:
            pass
        finally:
            proc.release()
    test_ns.remove()
    test.release()
    br100.remove().commit()
    ipdb.release()
```

After investigating this issue for a while...
if you're using wurstmeister/kafka with docker-compose, please notice that in Kafka's last version many parameters have been deprecated.
instead of using - 
```
KAFKA_HOST:
KAFKA_PORT: 9092
KAFKA_ADVERTISED_HOST_NAME: <IP-ADDRESS>
KAFKA_ADVERTISED_PORT: 9092
```
you need to use - 
```
KAFKA_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://:9092
KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://<IP-ADDRESS>:9092
```

view [this link](https://github.com/wurstmeister/kafka-docker/wiki/Connectivity) for more details

Influxdb seems to have a lot of issues if slashes or other non-word characters are in the key ([a-zA-Z0-9_]).

The proper command should be:

`influx -execute 'select "C:\\.used" FROM fs'` <--returns nothing

However the character for "ALL" works.

`influx -execute 'select * FROM fs'` <---returns all keys, showing that data is saved... just not accessible individually

Solution?:
Refrain from using non-word characters when naming keys. I don't really see a need for a colon and forward slash when the drive letter is all that's needed.

I guess I found the origin of the memory leak and it isn't the tablesorter core as I stated before - I forgot to remove the addition of the sticky headers to the tables while trying to uncover the memory leak origin.

As you already expect: It's the sticky header plugin or more precisely the removal of the sticky header plugin insinde the destroy function that causes the leak.

I found out that the detached DOM trees were referenced by the addHeaderResizeEvent and checked the usages of this function in the code afterwards.

The following call is made in the destroy function:
`ts.addHeaderResizeEvent(table, false);`

I then checked  the function itself and found out that the second parameter is named "disabled" and checked where it is used and found the following lines of code:

```
	if (disable) {
			wo.resize_flag = false;
			return false;
		}
```

I then changed the call in the destroy function to
`ts.addHeaderResizeEvent(table, true);`
and afterwards the memory leak was gone.

```python
from __future__ import (absolute_import, division, print_function,
                        unicode_literals)


import argparse
import datetime
import random

import backtrader as bt


class St(bt.Strategy):
    params = (
        ('printout', False),
        ('stake', 1000),
    )

    def __init__(self):
        pass

    def start(self):
        if self.p.printout:
            txtfields = list()
            txtfields.append('Len')
            txtfields.append('Datetime')
            txtfields.append('Open')
            txtfields.append('High')
            txtfields.append('Low')
            txtfields.append('Close')
            txtfields.append('Volume')
            txtfields.append('OpenInterest')
            print(','.join(txtfields))

    def next(self):
        if self.p.printout:
            # Print only 1st data ... is just a check that things are running
            txtfields = list()
            txtfields.append('%04d' % len(self))
            txtfields.append(self.data.datetime.datetime(0).isoformat())
            txtfields.append('%.2f' % self.data0.open[0])
            txtfields.append('%.2f' % self.data0.high[0])
            txtfields.append('%.2f' % self.data0.low[0])
            txtfields.append('%.2f' % self.data0.close[0])
            txtfields.append('%.2f' % self.data0.volume[0])
            txtfields.append('%.2f' % self.data0.openinterest[0])
            print(','.join(txtfields))

        # Data 0
        for data in self.datas:
            toss = random.randint(1, 10)
            curpos = self.getposition(data)
            if curpos.size:
                if toss > 5:
                    size = curpos.size // 2
                    self.sell(data=data, size=size)
                    if self.p.printout:
                        print('SELL {} @%{}'.format(size, data.close[0]))

            elif toss < 5:
                self.buy(data=data, size=self.p.stake)
                if self.p.printout:
                    print('BUY  {} @%{}'.format(self.p.stake, data.close[0]))


def runstrat(args=None):
    args = parse_args(args)

    cerebro = bt.Cerebro()
    cerebro.broker.set_cash(args.cash)

    dkwargs = dict()
    if args.fromdate:
        fromdate = datetime.datetime.strptime(args.fromdate, '%Y-%m-%d')
        dkwargs['fromdate'] = fromdate

    if args.todate:
        todate = datetime.datetime.strptime(args.todate, '%Y-%m-%d')
        dkwargs['todate'] = todate

    data0 = bt.feeds.BacktraderCSVData(dataname=args.data0, **dkwargs)
    cerebro.adddata(data0, name='Data0')

    data1 = bt.feeds.BacktraderCSVData(dataname=args.data1, **dkwargs)
    cerebro.adddata(data1, name='Data1')

    data2 = bt.feeds.BacktraderCSVData(dataname=args.data2, **dkwargs)
    cerebro.adddata(data2, name='Data2')

    cerebro.addstrategy(St, printout=args.printout)

    cerebro.addanalyzer(bt.analyzers.TimeReturn, timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Days)

    if not args.no_pyfolio:
        cerebro.addanalyzer(bt.analyzers.PyFolio, _name='pyfolio')

    results = cerebro.run()
    strat = results[0]

    # tr = strat.analyzers.getbyname('timereturn')
    tr = strat.analyzers.timereturn
    print('-- TIMERETURN:')
    for k, v in tr.get_analysis().items():
        print('{}: {}'.format(k, v))

    if not args.no_pyfolio:
        pyfoliozer = strat.analyzers.getbyname('pyfolio')

        returns, positions, transactions, gross_lev = pyfoliozer.get_pf_items()
        if args.printout:
            print('-- RETURNS')
            print(returns)
            print('-- POSITIONS')
            print(positions)
            print('-- TRANSACTIONS')
            print(transactions)
            print('-- GROSS LEVERAGE')
            print(gross_lev)

        if False:
            import pyfolio as pf
            pf.create_full_tear_sheet(
                returns,
                positions=positions,
                transactions=transactions,
                gross_lev=gross_lev,
                live_start_date='2005-05-01',
                round_trips=True)

    if args.plot:
        cerebro.plot(style=args.plot_style)


def parse_args(args=None):

    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        formatter_class=argparse.ArgumentDefaultsHelpFormatter,
        description='Sample for pivot point and cross plotting')

    parser.add_argument('--data0', required=False,
                        default='../../datas/yhoo-1996-2015.txt',
                        help='Data to be read in')

    parser.add_argument('--data1', required=False,
```

For simple packages which provide only `js` or only `css` or both but not images and or fonts this sounds cool, but as general approach is not because other files referenced by the css can be placed at the wrong place.

At the end it is responsibility of the package owner to structure it's package/library. For example Bootstrap has `css` and `js` in it's `dist` folder. Then if you set `NPM_STATIC_FILES_PREFIX` to `vendor` you will end up with `/static/vendor/bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css` and similar for the `js` which is cool.

Probably better improvement will be not only to tell which files the package you want in `NPM_FILE_PATTERNS` but also how to copy them in your `static`. Again with the example with the `bootstrap` I put the following:
```
NPM_FILE_PATTERNS = {
    'jquery': ['dist/*'],
    'bootstrap': ['dist/*']
}
```
And I ended up with `/static/vendor/jquery/dist/jquery.min.js` and I already said about `bootstrap`, but will be better if we can remove `dist` somehow from the resulting path.

Hi,

This is not possible. The only information provided to callbacks is the `Icon` instance.

This is by design; every menu item is supposed to provide its own handler. If you want to share code between handlers, and modify the behaviour slightly depending on the menu item, you can define the main handler and then call it with lambdas depending on the context:

    from PIL import Image, ImageDraw

    from pystray import Icon, Menu as menu, MenuItem as item


    def image(color1, color2, width=64, height=64):
        result = Image.new('RGB', (width, height), color1)
        dc = ImageDraw.Draw(result)

        dc.rectangle((width // 2, 0, width, height // 2), fill=color2)
        dc.rectangle((0, height // 2, width // 2, height), fill=color2)

        return result


    def on_activate(icon, quit):
        if quit:
            print('stopping...')
            icon.stop()
        else:
            print('doing nothing...')


    def setup(icon):
        icon.visible = True


    icon = Icon(
        'test',
        icon=image('white', 'black'),
        menu=menu(
            item(
                'Print',
                lambda icon: on_activate(icon, False)),
            menu.SEPARATOR,
            item(
                'Quit',
                lambda icon: on_activate(icon, True))))

    icon.run(setup)

In Python 3:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
```

hey @nicoddemus.

The main reason I use subclassing here is that it allows me to guarantee that subclasses don't break interfaces. For example, if I write:

```python
class SomeInterface(Interface):
    def method(self, x):
        pass

class MyImpl(implements(SomeInterface)):
    def method(self, x):  # correctly implemented
        pass

class MySubclass(MyImpl):
    def method(self, x, y):  # oops, this one is wrong
        pass
```

this package will raise an error telling you that `MySubclass` is failing to implement an interface that its parent promised to implement:

```
InvalidImplementation: 
class MySubclass failed to implement interface MyInterface:

The following methods of MyInterface were implemented with invalid signatures:
  - method(self, a, b) != method(self, a)
```

To get the same behavior with a decorator-based approach, you'd have to ensure that every subclass re-applies the same decorator. My general feeling is that the purpose of this library is to try to prevent these kinds of accidental mistakes, which I can do more effectively with the subclassing-based API.

The main downside to this strategy, as you note, is that my implementation requires insertion of an extra class, as well as a metaclass, which can cause conflicts if you're using another metaclass. You can generally work around this by making your metaclass a subclass of `ImplementsMeta`, but that's more metaprogramming than most people like to use for serious work. See https://github.com/ssanderson/interface/issues/12#issuecomment-357319120 for a concrete example of this.

I'd be open to the idea of an alternate decorator-based API that used less type-based magic at the cost of not protecting subclasses.

Now, the reason the single quote was replaced in the first place was for the following reason.

Reason for removal of ( ' ):
Computer (English) -> компьютер (Russian) -> komp'yuter (converted)
There is no ( ' ) in the Russian version, so we can't remove it prior to our process.
Once the process is complete, then we scan to see if there are any unwanted characters.
At this point we have (komp'yuter), which cannot be written as (komp-yuter) just like compu-ter is meaningless in English. (this was brought to my attention as I don't speak Russian)

One solution would be to split the ( ' ) lookup into two separate processes.
Preprocess: replace ( ' ) with ( - )
Postprocess: remove ( ' )

But even that might not work with the example above as they ( ' )s are escaped. We need to look for the possibility of also pre-process unsnapping, the manual way!

I think I know what you want now.

At present the value held by the proxy is effectively fixed, there is no way to automatically have its value changed.

One can manually update update the value held by the proxy by using:

```
proxy.__wrapped__ = newvalue
```

This isn't going to help you though because it would get too complicated to update potentially multiple proxy object instances if they existed, when changing a value in the key dictionary.

What I haven't investigated is what would happen if in a proxy class you replaced ``__wrapped__`` with a property, such that you could have a function be called when accessing it. It may work in case of pure Python implementation of proxy, but not the C version.

What this would be doing is allowing the wrapped value to be determined lazily.

The issue of the wrapped value being lazily evaluated has come up once before, but it was only for the very first lookup of it, not every lookup.

The problem with adding support for lazy evaluation is the overhead. It has potential for affecting the performance of the normal use case. For once only lazy evaluation it may not be so bad as once you have done it and got the value needed, can return to normal way of doing things. Having lazy evaluation on every access is still a problem.

Anyway, let me have a think about it. The once off lazy evaluation has been a request for a while and just haven't had time to work on it.

Yup, creating hidden services is the next step.

Just before the big 0.8.0 release, I added several messages with which the two sides can advertise their connectivity options to each other. There's an `abilities-v1` message which lists the kinds of outbound connections that the message-sender can make, so the first half is for Tor-capable clients to include a `{type: "tor-v1"}` element inthe `abilities-v1` array (see `wormhole.transit.Common.get_connection_abilities`).

The second half is for the other side to notice both 1: "oh, hey, they can connect to Tor" and 2: "Oh, hey, I can start an onion-service", and then actually start the onion service. Once the service is running and we know the `.onion` address, we send it in a `hints-v1` message (search for `get_connection_hints()`). On receipt, the node seeing the `hints-v1` message should initiate connections to all the hints it contains, one of which will be `{type: "tor-v1", hostname: "XYZ.onion", port: 1234}`.

One trick with onion services is that they take a fairly long time to spin up, so you don't want to bother doing if the other side won't be able to use it, or if there's a better pathway to use (e.g. if the sender is behind Tor, but the receiver has a public IP address, then the sender should just use Tor and an exit node to reach the receiver). Also, there's no point in having both sender *and* receiver spin up a hidden service: one is sufficient.

In most cases the sender is started before the receiver (the only exception being where you and I meet offline, agree on a code, then both go home and type it in). So there's a big chunk of idle time (after the sender starts, before the receiver finishes typing in the code) during which we could spin up the hidden service and not increase our overall latency. But we don't know if the receiver will be able to connect to it until after they startup and we get their first few messages.

So I'm thinking that maybe we should have a `wormhole send` argument that basically means "spin up the hidden service right away, just in case they end up being able to use it" (maybe this could be the same argument that says "only ever speak through Tor, because I want to protect my IP address"). Without this argument, we'll wait until the receiver tells us that they can speak Tor before we spin it up (and maybe we also look at the other connection hints, and exhaust all the more direct possibilities, before spinning up the HS).

(OTOH, I'm assuming that starting an HS imposes some load on the tor network, so it's polite to avoid doing it unless necessary.. maybe it's not that big of a deal, and we can just start one up every time we use `--tor`).

@JohnChang2014 Your image contains 76 layers, which is a lot. Docker is not able to prepare a tar archive with all these layers in time. And in time means here 10 minutes -- this is the default setting we use, see https://github.com/goldmann/docker-squash/blob/master/docs/environment_variables.adoc#docker_timeout. This tool tries to fetch the image 3 times and you end up with 30 minutes squash time which in reality is just waiting for Docker.

You have three options:

1. Use faster EC2 instance.
2. Optimize your image.
3. Bump timeout by setting the `DOCKER_TIMEOUT` env variable to something higher than 10 minutes.

Sure, specify the `DOCKER_TIMEOUT` environment variable: https://github.com/goldmann/docker-squash/blob/master/docs/environment_variables.adoc#docker_timeout

About usecases of ``async for`` - I think there are a lot of cases where you don't need to call ``cancel`` from another block of code. It's ok to let it cancel after you leave the ``async for`` block. It's not that hard to do (say with ``__del__`` method which works good in 3.5+). We can always cancel the coroutine also:

```python
async def start():
    queue = await connect_and_declare_queue()
    consumer = await queue.create_consumer(no_ack=True)
    async for msg in consumer:
        # Process message

consuming_task = asyncio.async(start())
# Somewhere else
consuming_task.cancel()  # We cancel the task here, not our consumer
```

And we can also leave the ``cancel`` method if that is a concern for the interface, or if you really hate to use ``__del__`` for closing the consumer:
```python
async def start():
    queue = await connect_and_declare_queue()
    consumer = await queue.create_consumer(no_ack=True)
    async for msg in consumer:  # This will just stop after consumer is cancelled
        # Process message
    # Somewhere else in anothe coroutine
    await consumer.cancel()
```

We can write the same use case in Python3.4 using ``get()`` function. It is the same proposal as I said above:

```python
@asyncio.coroutine
def start():
    queue = yield from connect_and_declare_queue()
    consumer = yield from queue.create_consumer(no_ack=True)
    while True:
        try:
            msg = yield from consumer.get()
        except asynqp.Cancelled:
            break
        # Process message
    # Somewhere else in code
    yield from consumer.cancel()
```

I had this issue for a long time with google-oauth2 as well and finally figured out the root cause of the problem last night.  I'm not sure if you are experiencing exactly the same problem, but for me it turned out to be an issue of conflicting apps.  

Apart from python-social-auth, we use django-session-security to automatically logout a user after a certain amount of time.  The security app stores the expiration time in the context data of the request, but psa's context processor switches all of that context data to be stored in a "backend" key during the complete step.  At this point, It looks like the base template that was rendered during this step would occasionally try to execute some javascript with context data that didn't exist, so the expiration time would be zero and the page would automatically reload. 

This was a tricky problem to debug because none of the error information seems to point to another app and it rarely happens because of the variable time that it will take javascript to begin execution before the page is redirected.  If the issue you're having is similar, look for any javascript in your code that executes a page reload and try to find if it depends on context data. If it does, you will just have to make sure that this code only runs if the context data exists.

```
from math import sqrt
from sklearn import neighbors
from os import listdir
from os.path import isdir, join, isfile, splitext
import pickle

import face_recognition
from face_recognition import face_locations
from face_recognition.cli import image_files_in_folder
X = []
y = []


ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS = {'png', 'jpg', 'jpeg'}

def train(train_dir, model_save_path = "", n_neighbors = None, knn_algo = 'ball_tree', verbose=False):
    """
    Trains a k-nearest neighbors classifier for face recognition.
    :param train_dir: directory that contains a sub-directory for each known person, with its name.
     (View in source code to see train_dir example tree structure)
     Structure:
        <train_dir>/
        ├── <person1>/
        │   ├── <somename1>.jpeg
        │   ├── <somename2>.jpeg
        │   ├── ...
        ├── <person2>/
        │   ├── <somename1>.jpeg
        │   └── <somename2>.jpeg
        └── ...
    :param model_save_path: (optional) path to save model of disk
    :param n_neighbors: (optional) number of neighbors to weigh in classification. Chosen automatically if not specified.
    :param knn_algo: (optional) underlying data structure to support knn.default is ball_tree
    :param verbose: verbosity of training
    :return: returns knn classifier that was trained on the given data.
    """
    
    for class_dir in listdir(train_dir):
        if not isdir(join(train_dir, class_dir)):
            continue
        for img_path in image_files_in_folder(join(train_dir, class_dir)):
            image = face_recognition.load_image_file(img_path)
            faces_bboxes = face_locations(image)
            if len(faces_bboxes) != 1:
                if verbose:
                    print("image {} not fit for training: {}".format(img_path, "didn't find a face" if len(faces_bboxes) < 1 else "found more than one face"))
                continue
            X.append(face_recognition.face_encodings(image, known_face_locations=faces_bboxes)[0])
            y.append(os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(img_path))[0])


    if n_neighbors is None:
        n_neighbors = int(round(sqrt(len(X))))
        if verbose:
            print("Chose n_neighbors automatically as:", n_neighbors)

    knn_clf = neighbors.KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=n_neighbors, algorithm=knn_algo, weights='distance')
    knn_clf.fit(X, y)

    if model_save_path != "":
        with open(model_save_path, 'wb') as f:
            pickle.dump(knn_clf, f)
    return knn_clf

def predict(X_img_path, knn_clf = None, model_save_path ="", DIST_THRESH = .5):
    """
    recognizes faces in given image, based on a trained knn classifier
    :param X_img_path: path to image to be recognized
    :param knn_clf: (optional) a knn classifier object. if not specified, model_save_path must be specified.
    :param model_save_path: (optional) path to a pickled knn classifier. if not specified, model_save_path must be knn_clf.
    :param DIST_THRESH: (optional) distance threshold in knn classification. the larger it is, the more chance of misclassifying an unknown person to a known one.
    :return: a list of names and face locations for the recognized faces in the image: [(name, bounding box), ...].
        For faces of unrecognized persons, the name 'N/A' will be passed.
    """

    if not isfile(X_img_path) or splitext(X_img_path)[1][1:] not in ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS:
        raise Exception("invalid image path: {}".format(X_img_path))

    if knn_clf is None and model_save_path == "":
        raise Exception("must supply knn classifier either thourgh knn_clf or model_save_path")

    if knn_clf is None:
        with open(model_save_path, 'rb') as f:
            knn_clf = pickle.load(f)

    X_img = face_recognition.load_image_file(X_img_path)
    X_faces_loc = face_locations(X_img)
    if len(X_faces_loc) == 0:
        return []

    faces_encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(X_img, known_face_locations=X_faces_loc)


    closest_distances,indices = knn_clf.kneighbors(faces_encodings, n_neighbors=1)

    is_recognized = [closest_distances[0][i][0] <= DIST_THRESH for i in range(len(X_faces_loc))]

    # predict classes and cull classifications that are not with high confidence
    for i in indices[0]:
        print(y[i])#This gives the names of the classdir.

def draw_preds(img_path, preds):
    """
    shows the face recognition results visually.
    :param img_path: path to image to be recognized
    :param preds: results of the predict function
    :return:
    """
    source_img = Image.open(img_path).convert("RGBA")
    draw = ImageDraw.Draw(source_img)
    for pred in preds:
        loc = pred[1]
        name = pred[0]
        # (top, right, bottom, left) => (left,top,right,bottom)
        draw.rectangle(((loc[3], loc[0]), (loc[1],loc[2])), outline="red")
        draw.text((loc[3], loc[0] - 30), name, font=ImageFont.truetype('Pillow/Tests/fonts/FreeMono.ttf', 30))
    source_img.show()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    knn_clf = train("knn_examples/train")
    for img_path in listdir("knn_examples/test"):
        preds = predict(join("knn_examples/test", img_path) ,knn_clf=knn_clf)
        print(preds)
        draw_preds(join("knn_examples/test", img_path), preds)
```

```python
from django.utils.formats import date_format

date_format('not a date')
```

The `MidiDispatcher.run` method runs in it's own thread, so that the MIDI output is decoupled from the MIDI input callback. They communicate via a queue. The rtmidi library calls the the class instance whenever a MIDI event arrives at the MIDI input and puts it in the queue. The loop in the `run` method listens for events on that queue and filters incoming events and then outputs the resulting events. This means that even while events are sent to the MIDI output, new events can be picked up from the MIDI input and put into the queue.

Hi @Richienb, you make some very good points about page load, and minimizing is indeed a very good practice. Our official documentation even minimizes our [styles and scripts](https://github.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/tree/master/docs/theme).

In general, the documentation isn't really meant to provide final, production quality styles and scripts, just simply to give people something that technically works and is a good starting point, so readable code with comments is much preferable. It is expected that the user may tweak and minimize for their own personal use.

It's pretty trivial for people to quickly minimize the examples as there are plenty of online minimizers for [CSS and JavaScript](https://www.minifier.org/), so providing minimized versions isn't really something I am currently aiming to provide.

I appreciate your interest and your contribution, and though I will probably pass on this particular pull, I look forward to any future contributions you may have that could be of benefit. Thanks for your interest.

I think I'm in favour of the current way things are implemented, partly because it would be a lot of work to change to the approach that you're suggesting. A lot of derivative implementations would become more complicated too, because using something that's compatible with the [standard complex derivative](http://mathworld.wolfram.com/ComplexDifferentiable.html) for holomorphic functions means that most derivatives extend to complex numbers without any need for a special case.

For example, at the moment the derivative of `y**2` is `2 * y`. If we used the approach that you're suggesting then the derivative of `y**2` would be `2 * np.conj(y)` for complex `y`, so you'd need to type check `y`. This is true for all standard nice math functions (which are holomorphic) — the derivative of any function composed of polynomials, exponentials, trig functions would need to be re-implemented to do type checking and to conjugate if the argument is complex.

How to implement Word2Vec implementation through  Autograd

Here's how you could do this for scalar to scalar functions:
```python
from autograd import grad_and_aux
from autograd.core import getval

def next_grad(gfun):
        def next_grad_fun(*args, **kwargs):
            top_grad, grads = gfun(*args, **kwargs)
            return top_grad, grads + [top_grad]
        return next_grad_fun

def derivs_up_to_order(fun, order):
    # Returns a function which computes and returns a list of the derivatives of
    # fun up to and including order.

    def dfun(*args, **kwargs):
        ans = fun(*args, **kwargs)
        return ans, [ans]

    for i in range(order):
        dfun = grad_and_aux(dfun)
        dfun = next_grad(dfun)
        
    def return_fun(*args, **kwargs):
        top_grad, grads = dfun(*args, **kwargs)
        return [getval(g) for g in grads]

    return return_fun
```
This would be efficient in the way that you describe. Example usage:
```python
In [3]: def f(x):
   ...:     return x**3
   ...:

In [4]: derivs_up_to_order(lambda x: x**3, order=5)(2.)
Out[4]: [8.0, 12.0, 12.0, 6.0, 0.0, 0.0]
```

We don't need to invalidate cache entries as soon as they're touched - we can wait until the end of a block to do that. In the case of a modification that requires immediate attention, a SimReliftException will be raised and [this code](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/factory.py#L96) can take care of it. Basically, juju core tries to consistently return UTC timestamps, but the timestamps are stored in mongodb as they come back from the machine, so it's possible that you have the case where someone is running a machine that isn't set to UTC, and a non UTC timestamp makes it back to us through the interface.

In light of that, I think that it's best to return timezone aware datetime objects.

It also makes for cleaner code, with less inline chomping of nanoseconds and such.

I figured out a way to make the camera work. It's probably not a great solution so won't be making a pull request, but if you want it to work, you can simply comment out some lines in platforms/android/camera.py as shown here:

```
# import android
# import android.activity
from os import unlink
from jnius import autoclass, cast
from plyer.facades import Camera
from plyer.platforms.android import activity

Intent = autoclass('android.content.Intent')
# PythonActivity = autoclass('org.renpy.android.PythonActivity')
MediaStore = autoclass('android.provider.MediaStore')
Uri = autoclass('android.net.Uri')


class AndroidCamera(Camera):

    def _take_picture(self, on_complete, filename=None):
        assert(on_complete is not None)
        self.on_complete = on_complete
        self.filename = filename
        # android.unbind(on_activity_result=self._on_activity_result)
        # android.bind(on_activity_result=self._on_activity_result)
        intent = Intent(MediaStore.ACTION_IMAGE_CAPTURE)
        uri = Uri.parse('file://' + filename)
        parcelable = cast('android.os.Parcelable', uri)
        intent.putExtra(MediaStore.EXTRA_OUTPUT, parcelable)
        activity.startActivityForResult(intent, 0x123)

    def _take_video(self, on_complete, filename=None):
        assert(on_complete is not None)
        self.on_complete = on_complete
        self.filename = filename
        # android.activity.unbind(on_activity_result=self._on_activity_result)
        # android.activity.bind(on_activity_result=self._on_activity_result)
        intent = Intent(MediaStore.ACTION_VIDEO_CAPTURE)
        uri = Uri.parse('file://' + filename)
        parcelable = cast('android.os.Parcelable', uri)
        intent.putExtra(MediaStore.EXTRA_OUTPUT, parcelable)

        # 0 = low quality, suitable for MMS messages,
        # 1 = high quality
        intent.putExtra(MediaStore.EXTRA_VIDEO_QUALITY, 1)
        activity.startActivityForResult(intent, 0x123)

    # def _on_activity_result(self, requestCode, resultCode, intent):
    #     if requestCode != 0x123:
    #         return
    #     android.activity.unbind(on_activity_result=self._on_activity_result)
    #     if self.on_complete(self.filename):
    #         self._unlink(self.filename)

    def _unlink(self, fn):
        try:
            unlink(fn)
        except Exception:
            pass


def instance():
    return AndroidCamera()
```

Checking for empty CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE must be done before project(), otherwise it will be already set to Debug. So the build was Debug by default, despite the "defaulting to Release" intent.

btw, the since version (8) in coming ONNX release is telling customers that onnx domain operator set has these new operators in version 8.

@techalchemy From a zoomed-out perspective, I can't see any reason why the parsing of `requirements.txt` and creation of `Pipfile` and `Pipefile.lock` should be at all related to the environment management.

In my opinion the great thing about `Pipenv` is its improved version management using `Pipfile` & `Pipfile.lock` *rather than* the virtualenv encapsulation. The main complaints about `Pipenv` that I've heard are because of people's misconceptions that it is only about environment management, and they want to keep their existing environment management solutions, but still use `Pipfile`.

This is sort of the case in my use-case. In my team, we run all our apps and their dependencies through a Docker image, so those dependencies needn't pollute the dev's system nor be effected by the existing state of the dev's system. In the context of this docker image, it doesn't make much sense to ask `Pipenv` to also create its own virtualenv inside the Docker image, since the Docker image is quite enough encapsulation (and in any case is running commands as root). This is why we use `Pipenv --system` is every case.

It would be really nice to be able to take advantage of `Pipenv`'s neat automatic behaviour with regard to `requirements.txt` files within our workflow. And I imagine that there are plenty of other contexts where people only want to use `Pipenv` with system `python` and would also appreciate it.

The problem is that the old version of the REST API expects the elastic pool name such as `pool1`, but the new version of the REST API expects full elastic pool resource id such as `/subscriptions/00000000-1111-2222-3333-444444444444/resourceGroups/rg/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/myserver/elasticPools/pool1`. 

In order to maintain compatibility of SQL CLI commands, I implemented a helper function that converts pool name to pool id ( https://github.com/azure/azure-cli/blob/dev/src/command_modules/azure-cli-sql/azure/cli/command_modules/sql/_validators.py#L87 ), however for `sql db copy` and `sql db replica create` commands this helper function uses the incorrect resource group and server name.

I am implementing a fix now. Until then it is possible to workaround which is to use the full resource id e.g. `/subscriptions/00000000-1111-2222-3333-444444444444/resourceGroups/rg/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/myserver/elasticPools/pool1`.

Hi @catap 

Sorry about the very late answer, I wasn't sure about the right answer until recently.

You can create the virtual machine extensions @EfrenRey gave to you using the "virtual_machine_extensions" property of your compute client. This is an example which use the parameters in the previous link

```python
params_create = azure.mgmt.compute.models.VirtualMachineExtension(
            location=self.region,
            name='MyCustomScriptExtension',
            publisher='Microsoft.Compute',
            extension_type='CustomScriptExtension',
            type_handler_version='1.7',
            auto_upgrade_minor_version=True,
            settings={
                "fileUris": [
                    "http: //Yourstorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net/customscriptfiles/start.ps1"
                 ]
            },
            protected_settings={
               "commandToExecute": "powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted -start.ps1",
               "storageAccountName": "yourStorageAccountName",
               "storageAccountKey": "yourStorageAccountKey"
             },
        )
        result_create = self.compute_client.virtual_machine_extensions.create_or_update(
            self.group_name,
            'MyVmName',
            params_create,
        )
```

I hope it helps, please tell me if it answers your question.
Thanks,

Hey @jpotts18!

As far as I'm concerned, this functionality "breaks the bitmap magic" when it comes to processing records from different dates, because each day the same uid is mapped to different "small values", and the processing of queries such as "how many users were active two days in a row, both yesterday and today" turns into a non-trivial exercise (not only in terms of programming, but mostly in terms of performance). In order to operate properly, bitmapist must keep user ids the same (bits must be set in the same positions in each Redis key).

There is a trick though. If you want to keep Redis memory under control, care more about "mass values" rather than individual values, then you can convert big uids (or even string uids, or whatever) to integers right from the Python with something like

```python
import random
bitmapist_uid = random.Random(my_long_uid).randint(1, 1000)
```

Depending on your case it may or may not be appropriate :)

Building d8fe06b on travis ci fails with `xgettext: language 'Desktop' unknown` because the gettext version used on the ubuntu 14.04 image used by travis ci is 0.18.3.1 and was released 2013-08-16. 
From what I can tell [the required version for .desktop](http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/gettext.git/commit/?h=v0.19.8.1&id=f83445c64c06b41c00ebbd71b0912f1d11c63919) support is 0.19.1 which was released on 2014-06-10. 

Ubuntu 16.04 already ships with 0.19.7, but unfortunately travis ci [is still working on 16.04 support](https://github.com/travis-ci/travis-ci/issues/7260#issuecomment-354350596) 

Since I assume very few people will try to build guake on ubuntu 14.04 I will just add ` || echo "Skipping .desktop files, is your gettext version < 0.19.1?"` warnings to the build process and fallback to not translating the .desktop files. This outputs should probably be removed after travis ci supports Ubuntu 16.04.

Basically in the code of Kinto we are sometimes building URLs.

 - https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/kinto/views/records.py#L35
 - https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/kinto/views/records.py#L47-L48
 - https://irccloud.mozilla.com/pastebin/69teBJGJ
 - https://irccloud.mozilla.com/pastebin/1bWBKhLm

Instead of building this URLs by hand we would like to use route_path to do it.

You can have a list of all the URLs and there name by running: ``.venv/bin/proutes config/kinto.ini`` (once you've [installed Kinto](http://kinto.readthedocs.org/en/latest/contributing.html#hack))

For instance ``'/buckets/%s/collections/%s' % (self.bucket_id, self.collection_id)`` could be written: ``route_path('collection-record', bucket_id=self.bucket_id, collection_id=self.collection_id)``
``route_path`` may return the ``/v1/`` prefix in front so we may have to create an helper that will remove it. But that's the idea.

The cache is on the resolver level, so its file-type-agnostic. The only reason a URL won't be cached at the moment is when HTTP request fails.

The background of caching was: Slow/Limited internet connection and not wanting to download the same multi-MB TIFF for each run of the tests. It's a very simple implementation, effectively 30 lines of code or so. The simplest "solution" would be to make the default **not** to cache.

The only solution to invalidate a cache entry for a URL is to delete the file corresponding to it in the cache directory. There is no programmatic way to do this.

We could add cache control functionality to the CLI, like `ocrd cache purge`, `ocrd cache rm FILE` etc. with little effort but it's still hacky.

The `nomkl` version of `numpy 1.10.4` requires `libgfortran 3.0`, so installing the wrong version of `libgfortran` forces conda to select a different version of `numpy`.

This is a known and still unresolved limitation. See https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6199 and related issues. The cause is `conda-forge::numpy` having `features: blas_openblas`. Those `features` are not well integrated into the dependency resolution.
Some workarounds:
* install `numpy` explicitly alongside `pytest-arraydiff`,
* explicitly install `conda-forge::numpy`,
* `conda config --env --add track_features blas_openblas`,
* `conda config --env --add pinned_packages conda-forge::numpy`,
* `conda install conda-forge::blas=1.1` (`blas=1.1` has `track_features: blas_openblas`)

@tomfleming We've had some fixes since `4.1.2` that might have tangentially touched on this, and may have ended up helping.  Can you see if you still have the problem as of `4.1.5`?

So, if I understand correctly, you're proposing that it be possible to activate more than one environment at a time by removing the deactivate step from the activate script.  Then you would add an option to deactivate that would allow it to deactivate all the previously activated environments.  Is that right?

Activating an environment prepends that environment's directories to PATH.  Thus activating two environments at the same time in the same terminal session is somewhat ambigous.  Which environment should have precedence?  Since we can't be ambigous about what environment is active, one environment can be active in a session at a time.

Each user should be able to activate a conda environment for each terminal session they have open.  Each terminal session has its own shell environment. For example, on my linux system, if I want to activate two conda environments, I need to open two terminal sessions.  Since each terminal session has its own shell environment, I can activate a different conda environment in each session without any problems.  There are no conda environments the need to be deactivated.

When using preinstalled environments, it might be a good idea to ensure somehow that users don't change the preinstalled conda environment.  One problem that can arise is when a user upgrades packages that breaks another users's code.  This is one of the several problems that conda environments solve very well.

I would, of course, highly encourage you to educate your users about conda environments.  Environments are pretty powerful and a little knowledge goes a long way.

If I've misunderstood your post, please let me know.  I think the above information should help you.

pip seems to use the following [template](https://github.com/pypa/pip/blob/9.0.1/pip/wheel.py#L414-L423) for entry points (at least when installing wheels):

``` python
maker.script_template = """# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import re
import sys

from %(module)s import %(import_name)s

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.argv[0] = re.sub(r'(-script\.pyw?|\.exe)?$', '', sys.argv[0])
    sys.exit(%(func)s())
"""
```

I would suggest that both conda and conda-build use this template as it seems the most general.  I will put together PRs implementing this in both projects.

I agree that we should not rely on package filenames. In this case, though, we don't have filenames - we have 'dists' which are hyphen-separated string representations of the package/version/build tuples. These have a lot in common with the 'standard' package filenames, but are distinct. Since we have dists, I'd prefer not to construct filenames from them and then consult the index; applying rsplit to extract the correct tuple component seems like the right approach give the input.

This can easily be made into a method of the Resolve class, but given that it doesn't need to consult the index, the advantage would mainly be one of scoping. I can go either way, just let me know what you'd prefer.

> I need the nodes have both id and score, what should I do?

You need to disable re-encoding decision trees, which is active by default. The re-encoding makes decision tree PMML files 50% smaller, but since the structure of the decision tree changes substantially, then node identifiers must be removed.

You can enable/disable decision tree conversion options using the `sklearn2pmml.pipeline.PMMLPipeline.configure(**pmml_options)` method:
https://github.com/jpmml/sklearn2pmml/blob/master/sklearn2pmml/pipeline/__init__.py#L58-L62

In the current case, do the following:
```Python
pipeline = PMMLPipeline([...])
pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
pipeline.configure(compact = False, flat = False, winner_id = True)
sklearn2pmml(pipeline, ...)
```

The last option (`winner_id = True`) also causes a special-purpose `OutputField` element to be generated.

The problem that django code and django based code can not know anything
about raw minio errors like NoSuchBucket.
So, it has no idea about those types and these exceptions can not be
explicitly caught there.
Such code also shall not know about those types.
We shall expose only standard exceptions that django has for it IOError is
one of them.
Anything else shall be re-packed.
I already have test cases code that relies on certain failures with
FilesystemStorage and S3  storage(whatever it is named)
and these tests fail with minio because of the mentioned issue.
Those exceptions are internal to minio client lib and they shall not
propagate beyond Storage.* calls

Jacob and Ben,
Ok, I've finally gotten around to looking at this again.
Using slices for the extract_diagonal_blocks() function was quite simple (I
think when I wrote this originally, I didn't realize you could just use the
same slicing syntax on the scipy sparse matrices).
As far as debugging standard interpolation, I see that for the 'bar'
example, we get zeros for the inner_denominator in ruge_stuben.h. This
means that when we sum over the connections between node k (a strongly
connected fine point) and the other strongly connected C points, we get
cancellation to zero.
This cannot happen if all off-diagonal elements are the same sign (as is
the case for M matrices). The 'bar' example matrix doesn't have this nice
property, however, so we can get cancellation.
I don't know whether there is a standard fix for this problem or not. I've
included John on this email to get his input here. As I understand it, the
idea is to account for the contribution from fine node k by spreading its
contribution to the other connected C points. The inner denominator sum
serves as part of a weighted averaging used when spreading node k's
contribution to the other C points. With this in mind, it sort of makes
sense to me that you could sum absolute values for the inner denominator
(thus avoiding cancellation). I'm not sure you'd end up with the
appropriate sign though, as I've not really thought much about the
interpolation weight formula in a non- M matrix context...
Thoughts from yall on this? Let me know if it's not clear what I'm talking
about. I'm sure John probably knows of some solution here.
Thanks,
Wayne

The given set of commands in this test case will sync a significant number of packages. For example, the command above will resolve to a page like http://mirror.fusioncloud.co/pub/centos/7.2.1511/os/x86_64/Packages/, which lists over 9,000 packages. It's unacceptable for an automated test to sync that many packages, especially from systems not hosted by us.

I'm not aware of any good technique for choosing which packages should be synced from a repository. The [yum importer documentation](http://docs.pulpproject.org/plugins/pulp_rpm/tech-reference/yum-plugins.html#yum-importer) does list a `skip` option, but:

* `skip` is a blacklisting tool. Should the set of content types change or be expanded, we'd unknowingly start syncing additional content. A whitelisting tool is needed.
* `skip` is a coarse tool. It lets one select certain types of packages, like RPMs and SRPMs, but not individual packages.

I don't think this bug is suitable for automated testing, given Pulp's current capabilities.

U2F is designed as a non-blocking protocol, and each request must immediately return a response without delay. The intention is to periodically poll the device when touch is needed, sending the same request repeatedly until it returns successful (or a timeout is reached, and the operation is aborted). Thus, when the device is in an idle state and is touched, it will keep a user present state for a short period of time, and return a successful response to a request that comes within that time.
