@Faldict That's awesome! The reason I was suggesting Docker was mostly because TensorRT integration also depends on Protobuf, which could make things slightly complex, depending on whether you already have it on your system or not. I'm glad a metal build just worked out for you as-is.  

Following the CI scripts, first you need to have Protobuf on your system, if you don't already. [This script](https://github.com/mkolod/incubator-mxnet/blob/tensorrt_integration/ci/docker/install/tensorrt.sh#L26:L45) shows you how. This also shows how to programmatically get TensorRT, rather going through the regwall (e.g. for automated builds, as is the case here). 

[Here](https://github.com/mkolod/incubator-mxnet/blob/tensorrt_integration/ci/docker/runtime_functions.sh#L439:L493) is the set of commands that the CI build is using for running CMake for [onnx-tensorrt](https://github.com/onnx/onnx-tensorrt), the make call for MXNet, etc.

The work that @KellenSunderland has recently done to enable CMake for the git submodules related to TensorRT (onnx-tensorrt, onnx, etc.) further simplifies the build recipe.

Please peruse the unit tests to get an idea of how to run with TensorRT for the Symbol API [here](https://github.com/mkolod/incubator-mxnet/blob/tensorrt_integration/tests/python/tensorrt/test_tensorrt_lenet5.py), and how to extract symbolic graphs from the Gluon models [here](https://github.com/mkolod/incubator-mxnet/blob/tensorrt_integration/tests/python/tensorrt/test_tensorrt_resnet_resnext_ssd.py).

An earlier version of the documentation can be found [here](https://github.com/mkolod/incubator-mxnet/blob/7f3d6ebd413d214815d25113b9e3ca78c69b0b43/docs/api/python/contrib/tensorrt.md). Even though the documentation is a bit stale and hence is not part of this PR, it is current enough to show the mechanics of adapting your models to use TensorRT inference. Up-to-date documentation will follow this PR once the release plan for the Docker image is known, to account for that aspect.

I did own research and I found following libraries/solutions:
- [BASS.NET](http://bass.radio42.com/) - .NET wrapper (P/Invoke) for multiplatform un4seen [BASS](http://www.un4seen.com/bass.html) audio library
- [ManagedBass](https://github.com/ManagedBass/ManagedBass) - also wrapper for BASS audio library, but with non restrictive license as BASS.NET. Additionallyall P/Invoke part is in PCL library.
- [OpenAL](https://www.openal.org/) and [OpenTK](https://github.com/opentk/opentk) - OpenAL is a cross-platform 3D audio API appropriate for use with gaming applications and many other types of audio applications. OpenTK is a C# wrapper for OpenAL and OpenGL.
- [Gstreamer#](http://www.mono-project.com/archived/gstreamer/) - I found this probably on stack overflow. Looks outdated. Looks like P/Invoke bindings to [gstreamer](https://gstreamer.freedesktop.org/) library. [Banshee](http://banshee.fm/download/development/) is a open source application which uses gstreamer under the hood.
- [Vorbis#](https://github.com/mono/csvorbis) - OggVorbis decoder written purely in C#. Not an audio library, but can be used with `System.Media.SoundPlayer` to play ogg files.
- [PortAudioSharp](https://code.google.com/archive/p/portaudiosharp/) - .NET bindings (P/Invoke) for the cross-platform low-latency audio library [PortAudio](http://www.portaudio.com/).
- [irrKlang](http://www.ambiera.com/irrklang/) - high level 2D and 3D cross platform (Windows, Mac OS X, Linux) sound engine and audio library.

Here's stack overflow question with some of this libraries mentioned: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35896/how-can-i-play-compressed-sound-files-in-c-sharp-in-a-portable-way

I tested only BASS.NET and ManagedBass and they are working with .NET Core on Mono.

Most of those C# libraries are only P/Invoke bindings to some multiplatform C library. I like ManagedBass because it has PCL library which is .NET Core compatible (DNX and DNX Core environments). BASS.NET (and probably other mentioned libraries) have only full .NET version, so I can run it only with `#if DNX451` switches and only in Mono runtime. With PCL ManagedBass I have code without `#if DNX451` switches which compiles properly for DNX and DNX Core version.

But there is one issue in DNX Core, from which reason I can run ManagedBass only on Mono. This issue is related with name of P/Invoked library. Binding in PCL library looks like that:
```[DllImport("bass", EntryPoint = "BASS_Start")]```
```public static extern bool Start();```
and because this is a PCL, then name of library in a code must be same for all platforms. But library name in various platforms is different, e.g. "libbass.so" in Linux and "bass.dll" in Windows.
Mono has automatic library mapping mechanism described [here](http://www.mono-project.com/docs/advanced/pinvoke/dllnotfoundexception/) (there is also manual mechanism described [here](http://www.mono-project.com/docs/advanced/pinvoke/dllmap/)).
Is there similar mechanism in DNX Core runtime or do you have plans for add it?

Just to have this on-record: I don't think it's a good idea to promote any other commands, at least for now.
The clean siloing of functionality by subservice becomes more beneficial the more services we add, and the chances for naming conflicts or confusion within a given subservice is highly unlikely.

We consider these two operations (endpoint search and ls) safe to promote because they operate in terms of one of our fundamental model objects, namely Endpoints. As such, the chances of a conflict with another service are exceedingly low.


However, getting back on my high-horse now, I think that requiring calls to a service to be done as explicit commands against that service is a very clean and natural design in the multi-service world. It also maps very well back onto the types of client objects with which a person is likely to interact if they need to move onto the SDK.

I noticed that we were re-building the extension module three times before actually running the tests...

It turns out

1) the code that checks if the extension module was newer than its source files was not there in our implementation of `build_ext.build_extension` method (I just copied it from cpython's [distutils](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/v3.6.2/Lib/distutils/command/build_ext.py#L485-L500))

2) there was a typo in the list of headers (a missing comma) which made it so that two headers' paths were combined into one, but the latter did not exist and thus distutils/setuptools would think the extension module was not up-to-date (as one of the dependencies was showing as missing), and would always run the `built_ext` command over and over.

In fact we use `node-browserify` to build a browserified version internally. Here's what we are doing (from Makefile):

``` make
browserify:
	if test ! `which browserify` ; then npm install browserify ; fi
	cp -r support/browserify/ ${TMP_DIR}
	browserify index.js -o ${TMP_DIR}/50_js-yaml.js
	cat ${TMP_DIR}/* > js-yaml.js
	rm -rf ${TMP_DIR}
	cp js-yaml.js demo/js/
```

In other words, we browserify js-yaml with node-browserify and then prepend and append to the result some portions of JavaScript that make us possible to add some browser-specific hacks and enclose everything to provide only one variable into global scope - `jsyaml`.

This is less straightforward than I thought.

Specifically, we can only elide writes to the snapshot on same steps if the dependencies for a resource have not changed. For example, consider the following case:

Resources are described as `ID(dependencies)`.
Base order: `A(nil), B(A)`
Diff order: `B(nil), A(B)`

The base snapshot will have the following contents in order: `[A, B]`.
Assuming that the inputs to B did not change in the diff program, the snapshot manager will see the following events:
`SameStep(B), SameStep(A)`
If the snapshot is not written when the same steps are processed and the program terminates without ever writing the snapshot (e.g. because it crashes), the order of these resources in the snapshot will be incorrect, as the snapshot will retain the original ordering.

`.def` doesn't work with a `std::function<...>`, primarily because the core functionality doesn't include the stl header.  I suppose it would be possible to add it, in theory, but it's actually pretty easy to work around it by binding it as an attribute rather than a function:

```C++
.def_readonly("getNr", &Stuff::getNr)
```

Or you can use `def_readwrite` if you want the Python side to be able to replace the function, like this:

```C++
.def_readwrite("getNr", &Stuff::getNr)
.def("call_it", [](Stuff &self) { return self.getNr(); })  // For the example below
```

```Python
>>> a = Stuff(2)
>>> a.getNr()
2.0
>>> a.getNr = lambda: 123.0
>>> a.getNr()  # Via Python binding
123.0
>>> a.call_it()  # Via C++ invoking the `std::function` directly
123.0
```

But I am wondering if concurrent signature checking is still urgent. Right now steps are checked and executed concurrently (if `concurrent=True`) but not if tasks are involved. The idea is that tasks can only be submitted from the master process and steps involving tasks usually do not have anything between `output` and `task`, so running 

```
input: concurrent=True
task:
```
basically starts a bunch of processes and does nothing. Checking existing signature is about the only thing that is useful in subprocesses.

I have just now pushed a solution to this problem d4c10d351. Short explanation:

when the callback registered to atexit is invoked disable will call back to python land and write some more information to the log. This did not succeed in the case of atexit.

I fixed that by using the code that is used by pypy to write back that information in C and avoid the callback to Python.

This should not have affected the usual invocation:

`$ python -m vmprof script.py`

Hello @jbonzo and thank you for reaching us! So let me explain our API, since we've changed it a bit in the last months and unfortunately the documentation may need a revamp. Our API is mainly composed by:
* `Tracer.trace()`: is our main API and the way to go when you need to instrument your framework, library or application (manual instrumentation). It uses our in-process context propagation to find the current active `Span`. In that way, you don't need to move your `Span` reference around the code to set the `child_of=` parameter. It will be automatically inherited from our propagation system.
* `Tracer.start_span()`: is the manual API that was here to mimic the same API from the [OpenTracing specification][1]. Because in some situations it's possible that the propagation system has troubles to find out the current active `Span` (think about complex applications with a lot of asynchronous calls like `asyncio` or Tornado), this API ignores all the above and allows you to easily define your hierarchy. It is intended to be used only to cover some edge cases and, indeed, it doesn't use the automatic in-process propagation.
* the `Context` class is our internal machinery to keep track of the current active `Span`, the current trace, and the current `parent_span_id` / `parent_trace_id` (this is recently added and yet not released). It's very uncommon to use that object directly and indeed it's not documented because no one should use that directly when creating their traces. It's created, handled and propagated by the above APIs.

About your example `start_span(child_of=tracer.get_call_context()`, it's actually working but what you're trying to achieve is already handled by the other API. Indeed,`Tracer.trace()` is the way to go and `Tracer.start_span()` is here only for specific edge cases where manual handling is required.

About `context.add_span()`, it's still part of our internal machinery and it should not be used directly (hence it's not documented in our examples). So to summarize:
* always use `Tracer.trace()` as a way to go since it uses in-process context propagation making everything easier
* in some edge cases you may use `Tracer.start_span()` to define your hierarchy, though it will not inherit any `Span` unless you specify that
* don't use the `Context` class directly, but let the library handle that

Hope it helps and feel free to ask more questions if you need!

[1]: https://github.com/opentracing/opentracing-python/blob/a86ee3d49df569691433e0ce0101105638ca8be8/opentracing/tracer.py#L44-L49

@rogaha I certainly agree `atexit` gives you stronger guarantees at exit than just `__del__`. Nevertheless, I find that `__del__` usually works fine (as long as one's careful to avoid any circular references) and it delivers the most intuitive behaviour (where with "intuitive" I guess I mean "pythonic").

I'd like to see that we can write `p = my_topic.get_producer()` in any context, and that we may then expect that soon after `p` goes out of scope, it gets flushed, stopped and deleted. That would make it most similar to `f = open("filename")`: that is also usually taught with the caveat that one should always `f.close()` (just like `p.stop()`) or that if possible one should use `with open("x") as f:`, but nevertheless few people heed that advice and everyone sort of expects that `f` gets closed anyway after it goes out of scope.

Having `atexit` baked into the class breaks that expectation, because the registry will keep the instance alive - so with that, `p` no longer gets flushed and stopped if it goes out of scope. If you register it yourself, because you want those extra-strong guarantees, then it's different, because it's a conscious choice and so you won't expect automatic garbage-collection to work. That would only add an extra line when you create your instance:
```
producer = topic.get_producer()
register_cleanup_func(producer)
```
(where `register_cleanup_func` is a slightly different implementation of what you have here). This is roughly what I meant with having it as an external facility.

During my call with @pconrad-fb I was reminded about all the things that are weird with our setup and git, so I'm going to attempt to write them all up.
The files that control the instruction generator (https://certbot.eff.org/) are located in the certbot/website repository at: https://github.com/certbot/website/tree/master/_scripts/instruction-widget
The files that control the documentation (https://certbot.eff.org/docs/) are located in the certbot/certbot repository and get automagically pulled into the website when it's deployed: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/tree/master/docs
Since none of you will have write permissions for either of these repositories, you'll have to go through the work flow of:

- Creating a personal fork of the repo - associated with your github account - for both of these repositories (certbot/certbot and certbot/website)
- Clone both of these to your local machine
- Make local changes
- Push these local changes up to your github tracked fork (git push --set-upstream origin my_new_branch_name)
- Make a pull request from your fork's branch to the appropriate master branch (e.g. certbot/certbot:master)

Then we'll review those changes, comment on them, and merge them when they look good. I also strongly suggest making different local branches for each of these changes, so that as we comment on certain change sets you can edit them without preventing other changes from getting merged.

> I'll leave it open though since it's a bug that it gives the error at all since --keep-until-expiring means that it shouldn't raise a "Cert not yet due for renewal" problem, and then shouldn't exit uncleanly.

Printing "Cert not due for renewal" is not a bug, its the intended behavior. Just to make sure we're on the same page, the help for `--keep-until-expiring` is shown below:
```
  --keep-until-expiring, --keep, --reinstall
                        If the requested cert matches an existing cert, always
                        keep the existing one until it is due for renewal (for
                        the 'run' subcommand this means reinstall the existing
                        cert) (default: False)
```
The addition of this flag causes the client to check whether or not the certificate is due for renewal. If it is, the client obtains a new cert, otherwise, you'll see the message "Cert not yet due for renewal" and the client will exit (potentially after reinstalling the certificate if using the `run` subcommand).

Printing this message does not cause the client to exit with a non-zero status. In your log above, the reason the client exited with a non-zero status is because it tried to open a ncurses dialog. 

Are you trying to run the client automatically using something like `cron`? Doing so would explain the Python dialog error. If so, I encourage you to look at our [renewal documentation](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewal) specifically the part about using the `-q/--quiet` flag to only provide error output.

Hope this helps!

Yes, indeed this is the behavior since version 0.19. Because of various errors while trying to parse inactive vhost files in some configurations (see #3545 #3791). Certbot now only sees the same vhosts that Apache does.

To make Certbot version >= 0.19 detect your vhosts, you can enable them prior to running Certbot by issuing: 
`a2ensite nameofsitetoenable`

or by symlinking the vhost file to `/etc/apache2/sites-enabled` by:

`X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set1_ip_asc` is a symbol added in 1.0.2-beta2. If it's failing at runtime then the balance of probability is that when compiling against 1.0.2 you didn't set it up to properly look for that version of the library. 1.0.2, 1.0.1, and 1.0.0 all have the same soversion by default so the dynamic linker is almost certainly just loading the 1.0.1 version from your default Ubuntu paths. You can probably confirm this by doing an `ldd` on the `_openssl.so`. You'll probably see something like:

```
	libssl.so.1.0.0 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libssl.so.1.0.0 (0x00007ff647c04000)
	libcrypto.so.1.0.0 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcrypto.so.1.0.0 (0x00007ff647828000)
```

When what you really want to see is

```
	libssl.so.1.0.0 => /usr/local/openssl/lib/libssl.so.1.0.0
	libcrypto.so.1.0.0 => /usr/local/openssl/lib/libcrypto.so.1.0.0
```

or whatever the path to your 1.0.2 install is.

It is non-trivial and tricky to build a custom OpenSSL and have libraries link against it successfully in Linux. I recommend against it unless you are familiar with linker args like `rpath` or comfortable with altering soversions.

Thanks for the detailed post @davidjb.

> Is it appropriate for Certbot to be creating those files and setting the SELinux context after reconfiguring Nginx (but before restarting it) or is that out of scope?

Personally I think that Certbot trying to reconfigure an arbitrary SELinux setup so it works properly is complicated and out of scope. What I'd like to see is the packages on systems where SELinux is enabled be modified so Certbot works properly. This can be done using the conventions of the distro rather than Certbot trying to handle any arbitrary configuration.

> Either way, Certbot would be best to confirm that Nginx restarts okay after reconfiguration, which it doesn't appear to do at present. In my case, nginx -t succeeded in the console (because SELinux doesn't apply at that point) but an actual systemctl restart nginx failed with permission denied on the /var/lib/letsencrypt/*.log files.

Since not all systems use systemd (at least not yet), Certbot doesn't use it to start nginx. Instead, it uses the command `nginx -c <path to nginx server root> -s reload`. If nginx isn't already running, it uses `nginx -c <path to nginx server root>`. I'm assuming at least the former command doesn't fail with this setup? If you have a suggestion for a reliable way we can catch this problem earlier without relying on distro specifics, we'd certainly be open to using that rather our current approach. I'm currently not aware of a way to do that.

> Finally, for note the issue wasn't a problem on the test CA; that was issuing certificates fine. I haven't looked into that side of things but I suspect the authorisation was cached or something similar, unless the test configuration generates a different nginx conf that doesn't touch these log files.

Certbot also configures nginx to use these files when getting certs from Let's Encrypt's staging server, but Let's Encrypt does [reuse authorizations](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/upcoming-change-valid-authz-reuse/16982) when you still have a valid one for that domain tied to your account. I suspect this was the cause of the behavior you were seeing. Certbot sets up the challenge, but because Let's Encrypt didn't verify it, you got a certificate just fine.

For each certificate that was successfully renewed (that is there was a new certificate actually issued), `--renew-hook` is called again. It is not called for certificates that were not actually renewed. So for example if there are no renewed certificates, `--renew-hook` is not called at all.

This is actively unwise, I'm afraid. This is because exporting the certificates using `security` doesn't properly respect the trust usage settings in OS X, which means that you will choose to trust certificates that have been explicitly marked as untrusted. 

The reason for the system Python behaviour is because OS X's system OpenSSL is patched to call into the OS.

I am working on a tool that will do this properly, available as [python-hyper/certitude](https://github.com/python-hyper/certitude). It currently requires a Rust compiler but otherwise works fine. I'm aiming to productise that into wheels shortly.

That's definitely not a dependency. What appears to have happened is that jupyter_declarativewidgets was installed at some point on your system, and was registered as a notebook server extension. Subsequently, you have set up an env without it, or otherwise uninstalled it, but it's still registered as an extension in config. What's failing is the loading of the extension (that's the `init_server_extensions` part). You can look for `jupyter_declarativewidgets` in `~/.jupyter/jupyter_notebook_config.{py,json}` and see where it is added to your configuration, and remove it there.

@Franck-Dernoncourt This is actually not a bug, as the current epoch is computed on base of your current parameters and the snapshot-persisted global step-count. Take a close look into this excerpt:

```Python
# Number of GPUs per worker - fixed for now by local reality or cluster setup
gpus_per_worker = len(available_devices)

# Number of batches processed per job per worker
batches_per_job  = gpus_per_worker * max(1, FLAGS.iters_per_worker)

# Number of batches per global step
batches_per_step = gpus_per_worker * max(1, FLAGS.replicas_to_agg)

# Number of global steps per epoch - to be at least 1
steps_per_epoch = max(1, model_feeder.train.total_batches // batches_per_step)

# The start epoch of our training
# Number of GPUs per worker - fixed for now by local reality or cluster setup
gpus_per_worker = len(available_devices)

# Number of batches processed per job per worker
batches_per_job  = gpus_per_worker * max(1, FLAGS.iters_per_worker)

# Number of batches per global step
batches_per_step = gpus_per_worker * max(1, FLAGS.replicas_to_agg)

# Number of global steps per epoch - to be at least 1
steps_per_epoch = max(1, model_feeder.train.total_batches // batches_per_step)

# The start epoch of our training
self._epoch = step // steps_per_epoch
```
So what happens is that your training-set-size during training differs from your current training-set size. Thus the strange epoch number.

Simplified example (without confusing batch size): 
If you once trained 5 epochs of a 1000 sample training-set, you got 5000 "global steps" (persisted as a number in your snapshot).
After this training you change the command line parameters to a set of size 1 (your `--limit_*` parameters).
"Suddenly" you'll get displayed epoch 5000, because 5000 global steps means applying a data-set of size 1 5000 times.

Yeah one epoch takes about 11h. GPU utilization is about 99% for the entire time.

The logic behind the hyperparameters is mostly hard-won trial an error. The most expensive parameter to tune was the dropout rate which is much higher than that used in the original Deep Speech paper. So we had to do a binary search using the TED data set which took lots of compute time.

Hmm. My concern is that many NLP datasets are just too large to be kept in memory at all times. Particularly since python representations of dicts and string are large memory-wise. I don't feel like torchtext acknowledges this. We can hack our own thing, but I would prefer for the design of the library to reflect the following use case: 

1) Stream data to construct fields. 
2) Write data to disk in some compressed form. 
3) Stream data out again to convert to batches.

This has been the case at least 3-4 times for me already, which means that supporting those extension layers officially in torchvision at the moment would be hard to maintain -- if the user updates PyTorch, torchvision breaks, if the user update torchvision but not pytorch, it also breaks, he needs to update both at the same time. This was a recurring issue with Lua-Torch, and I'd rather avoid it at the moment.

About where to put the aforementioned layers, I'm not yet convinced on what is the right solution.
In the one hand, those backward-compatibility issues makes me hesitant to put them in torchvision (as it's widely used and up to now has been a python-only lib), so putting them in Detectron would make sense.
On the other hand, having a unified place where the basic building blocks can be found is a nice thing to have.
I think once we release Detectron, we might converge into gradually migrating a few layers and abstractions to torchvision, as the BC breakages on the C++ level would be less recurrent I'd hope.

This is expected and for me, usually a good behaviour. Some things to keep in mind:

 * In some sense a failed task is also considered pending. It will be retried once (default 15 minute) cooldown is over.
 * Luigi also has the concept of disabled task. It's not on by default as it complicates stuff for the first-time users of luigi, but you should consider using this, because disabled tasks are not considered pending, and you worker will happily quit as it believes there's nothing to wait for.

My suggestion would be to consider if configuring disabling of tasks could work for you. Let me know it it works out.

hi @jleclanche I'm going through the issues now and trying to understand this one. Pipenv has changed a **lot** since this plugin was written, namely in the way it now plays nicely inside virtualenvironments.

Thanks for raising the issue

The current flag is `--travis-after`. So something like `--travis-detect`, `--travis-envlist`, `--travis-detect-envlist` would be appropriate (and/or the negative). `--travis-envlist` seems fine to me, which would make naming the files `envlist` in #83 just fine.

----

There's one more case that I just thought of to consider, and that's if we wanted to add options to match these flags in the `[travis]` config section. I can think of two ways that might look:

```ini
[travis]
envlist = False
after = True
```

This makes sense, but conflicts with an idea that's been floated before of using the `envlist` key as an override for the global envlist from Tox, instead of just detecting the envs that tox knows about. I can't think of a better spelling for that use, or else I'd be happy to move forward with this one.

```ini
[travis]
features =
  envlist: False
  after: True
```

This solves the conflicting key problem, but I think it looks ugly, and it would be more annoying to parse.

What do you think?

The main thing that bothers me is that neither epytext nor ReST has a way to nest `:param:` descriptions, but I admit that the interface is just a non-ideal tradeoff in a different direction: we really are just describing the parameter to just this one function, not a general interface that can be passed all kinds of places, so making the user jump out and read a different class's docstring is unpleasant in a different way than having ad-hoc parameter descriptions instead of formal markup.

I was investigating on this issue and I found out that the default property should be `"true"` (with quotes) in your fist example and then it would work. But I tried to run locally to reproduce the fix, but I did not worked. So I dig into more on that issue...

I found out that the Swagger model converts the `default` property to a [native boolean value](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-core/blob/master/modules/swagger-models/src/main/java/io/swagger/models/parameters/AbstractSerializableParameter.java#L198). And since the Connexion Codegen [simply serializes the swagger definition to `yml`](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-codegen/blob/master/modules/swagger-codegen/src/main/java/io/swagger/codegen/languages/FlaskConnexionCodegen.java#L298) to be used when running the app by Connexion it breaks.

If you change the generated `yml` default value to `"true"` (with quotes) it works.

I guess that is a major issue in [all generators that output swagger definitions like that](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-codegen/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=Yaml.mapper%28%29.writeValueAsString).

Nikolaj is right about the time being taken up in the simplifier; the conversion of the _real_ number 1.189731495357231765E4932 to a long double float is expensive because it's in base-10 (E) and floats are in base-2, and the exponent is a large number. The current implementation can perhaps be improved (but I won't be able to look into the details of that in the next few days) and we'll have to look into ways to make sure the timeouts are at least respected.

I would recommend not to construct a real numbered term at all, but to keep everything in floats whereever possible. This particular mode of operation (large numerals) can be improved, but the other (symbolic) conversions from/to reals will create very ugly non-linear constraints that will be very hard to solve (real-to-float) or they will cause the non-linear real solver will give up and return unknown (float-to-real). The default/usual mode of operation is to use 3 bit-vectors to construct float numerals (this is true for all of SMT but also for Z3), and that is always fast.

- Right, inc-ref and dec-ref rely on the ast conversion.

- There are rather many functions that take Z3_ast objects as arguments and we assume they are expressions. This isn't documented directly. The higher-level APIs get the separation in a more systematic way and can be used as guides. 
More to the point of usability, the C++ API is so much more convenient to use and resilient against low level errors. Only environments that can't or will not benefit from C++ should have to deal with the C API. As for documentation, improvements continuously remain possible.

The TravisCI infrastructure is designed to make it easy to reproduce build failures by using Docker.

https://travis-ci.org/Z3Prover/z3/jobs/288109767

To reproduce the above failed build you can run this on a Linux machine.

```
LINUX_BASE=ubuntu_16.04 \
C_COMPILER=/usr/bin/gcc-5 \
CXX_COMPILER=/usr/bin/g++-5 \
TARGET_ARCH=x86_64 \
Z3_BUILD_TYPE=Debug \
RUN_UNIT_TESTS=BUILD_ONLY \
contrib/ci/scripts/travis_ci_linux_entry_point.sh
```

This is documented in https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/contrib/ci/README.md


Seeing the failure is with the regression tests you can change `RUN_UNIT_TEST` to `SKIP` to speed things up. You will need to have Docker installed for this to work.

The way I've been debugging failures is letting this script run until it hits a failure. When this happens I then create a new interactive container that starts at last successful `RUN` command in the docker file (just run `docker images` and see which one is the newest).

```
docker run --rm -ti <image_id>
```

In this environment you can use `sudo` without a password to install any debugging dependencies you might need (e.g. gdb). To use gdb inside the container you might need to pass `--privileged` so that it has permission to use ptrace.

Regarding reload/runserver, perhaps I have a compromise that everyone can work with:

watchdog is old and crusty and buggy, I want to rebuild it with python 3.6 and proper support for asyncio. If that works it should obviously be a different package. It would have an interface as simple as (I'm guessing):

```python
from watcher import Watcher

async def main():
    async for event in Watcher('/path/to/dir', filter='*.py', debounce=0.1):
        <do stuff when events occur>
```

Advantages:
* `aiohttp-devtools` would remain in one place, would get simpler and hopefully #118 would be fixed
* those who don't want to use `aiohttp-devtools` could get their code reloading on files changes very easily. But most people who just want an out-of-the-box way to run their aiohttp app in development wouldn't need to change anything or make a choice about which package to use.
* the package would have no dependency on aiohttp so would be more widely useful.

We could also remove jinja2 (which is required by the `start` command only) as a required dependency of `aiohttp-devtools` and allow people to install it with `aiohttp-devtools[start]`. That should please people who don't want to install an extra package for a subcommand they never use.

- https://github.com/airbrake/airbrake-ruby#airbrakeadd_filter where we add a user-supplied callback that does more complex things.

Not that it matters really, as it doesn't really impact the final outcome, but the parser doesn't really keep track of what the separators are. It doesn't work by trying a bunch of different date formats, it tokenizes the strings and infers what the parts of a datetime format are. `YYYY.DD.MM` vs `YYYY-DD-MM` are more or less equivalent to the parser.

Honestly, if you have some ISO dates and some dates of an unknown format, you may want to just try parsing it with `strptime` with an ISO format first, then if it errors, run the parser on it.

@jbrockmendel It's definitely intended to be external-facing.

I honestly don't see a big problem with diverging from `datetime.datetime.today` - `datetime.datetime.today` is an *alternate constructor* for `datetime`. This is a utility that gives you today at midnight. To me, the main problem with the name is mostly that it doesn't convey that it's going to give you a `datetime` back just from the name. I think it will be far less confusing than Python's current behavior.

> Note there is a small difference: now will accept a tzinfo argument.

Wow, I didn't even realize this. So it's basically a *worse* version of `now()`. I don't think we should be emulating this API at all.

> After timelex.split the parser doesn't recombine tokens. It occasionally examines neighboring tokens for contextual inference, but assumes that each individual token is "right". e.g. all of the "is this a valid XYZ?" checking is based on "is this token included in the dictionary for XYZ?"

Hmm, I guess I assumed that the `0.5` stuff was being tokenized, but I can see now that it's explicitly allowed for. In fact, some of the tokenization stuff is already happening in the parser.

I suppose I can't think of any reason why it matters if `A/B/C` is considered a single token, though if you look at [this approach](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/pull/227/files#diff-ed798069162e7ab7db9a45da6c4a076fR996), it seems a little better to me because they're building the string up dynamically based on whether it matches prefixes in the time zone list.

I think the only reason I didn't merge that one was because I wanted to convert the lookup table into a radix tree instead of whatever it is now.

> Short-medium term, I'm not sure how to do bug-fixes that aren't "tweaks around the edges".

Yes. I think there have been a few, like the `%b` one and such, but my point is that the biggest "bug" that people care about it #402, which was actually caused by some naive changes to an untested and undocumented feature of the weird interface. Probably the second most important bug is #214. Probably the third biggest problem with the parser is that it is *slow*. The first two are interface decisions, and I think all three of them require that we have a good understanding of the fundamental mode of operation of the parser. Not that it's not valuable to fix these bugs, but I'm hesitant to make any *significant* changes to the mode of operation  and, more importantly, to the public interface and public behavior, because I don't want to constrain our future decisions about interface design.

> > as we seem to have markedly different sensibilities about what makes for a good API.
>
> I'm chalking this up to a learning experience, being the junior team-member and figuring out how to work around a sometimes-frustrating situation. I appreciate you bearing with me as I get up the learning curve.

To be honest, I suspect it's that you seem to do a lot of work with `pandas`, which has an "easy" API that works well for interactive programming, but in the end it's quite unwieldy and adds a lot of complexity and overhead - both from a maintenance perspective and at runtime. In general, I prefer `dateutil` to be closer to the standard library, and to have, as much as possible, a clean API with separation of concerns, etc. `pandas` also has a sort of "advantage" in that they make no real claims about their stability. They've been on `0.x` for *years* (and in semver `0.x` has the special meaning of being alpha - so even minor releases can break backwards compatibility).

Either way, it's a totally different API design philosophy.

> Is using timelex.split even necessary here?

This is kinda what I was thinking. It's a little weird that `_timelex` is being reused for this purpose.

My thoughts on negative timedeltas:

- Another way to handle negative numbers is to just convert the positive timedelta and stick a negative sign in front of the lot.
- In fact pandas must have some rules as to how they format timedeltas; see http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/timedeltas.html#reductions where they put the minus sign in front of the largest number
- I distinctly remember being personally very confused by that way of doing stuff in some cases. A `timedelta(seconds=-1)` would be printed as `-1 days +23:59:59:00` which at first glance just looks weird for such a simple timedelta in my opinion.

`itsdb.get_data_specifier()` calls `itsdb._split_cols()` which has this line (which is the heart of the problem):

```python
    return list(map(str.strip, colstring.split('@')))
```

`str` refers to different types in Python2 and Python3, so `str.strip()` may not work with `unicode` objects in Python2 or `bytes` objects in Python3. Rather than specify `str.strip` you can rely on duck-typing to ignore the difference and strip spaces no matter what type each column value is.

What you're doing here is not talking to two separate drones -  but taking to one drone twice from separate dronekit instances. - support multiple keys in the same middleware.
- incur overhead of multiple middlewares and keep each middleware isolated to a single key.

I'm inclined to go with multiple keys in the same middleware option since it **should** be very clean to implement and the overhead could get large-ish if someone say wants 100+ keys for in flight signing.

At first I was inclined to say that's a minority use case but here are two use cases which might necessitate that.  Both are different versions of the same use case.

1. The execution client for ethereum alarm clock sets up multiple accounts.  This allows the client to issue many execution transactions which aren't dependent on one another due to nonces to execute scheduled transactions that are all scheduled at about the same time.  **if** the alarm service ends up with a lot of usage, it'd be reasonable to have 50-100 accounts being used for execution.
2. Programatic trading on a decentralized exchange.  Same issue, wanting to be able to issue orders concurrently that aren't nonce dependent.

The Viterbi invariant that V[t, i] contains the optimal state sequence for observations 1 to t-th ending in state i. To get the optimal path for T observations you

* compute V[t, i] for all t and i;
* take the argmax for t = T (last observation)
* and backtrack from there to obtain the path. 

Note that the resulting path is not necessarily a subpath of the optimal path for T + 1 observations, because to compute V[T + 1, i] you would use the whole row V[T, i] and not just the argmax(V[T, i]).

I don't think that `SECRET_KEY` is intended to be of type `bytes`.

The default, empty value is `str`, not `bytes`. The value generated by `startproject` is also `str`, not `bytes`.

https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/settings/#std:setting-SECRET_KEY doesn't say anything about `bytes`.

If you look at the implementation of `get_random_string`, it assumes the opposite (in a rarely use code path).

In a 12-factor app it's a best practice to provide secrets via environment variables and random bytes (half of which are non-ASCII) aren't safe to pass via environment variables.

That said, it's best not to crash when SECRET_KEY contains `bytes`. That's a good change.

> Are there any ways to find the sense of the word if its not given already? Also is it possible to find those words in the first place?

Well, in that case, you can use spaCy's part-of-speech tags, which should be fairly accurate. Using the sense2vec spaCy extension also makes sense then, because it lets you get the sense2vec vector straight from the token.

> Also is it possible to find those words in the first place?

Do you mean, if you have a bunch of text and want to find homonyms in it? You can always load in [existing lists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_true_homonyms), use the [`Matcher`](http://spacy.io/usage/linguistic-features#rule-based-matching) to find them and then compare the POS tags etc. You could also iterate over the tokens in your text, find tokens that have the same `token.text` and then compare them (and store their `Doc` or sentence `Span` if you've found the same token text with different POS tags).

(Just keep in mind that models are statistical and the tagger can always make mistakes, too – especially for words that are ambiguous like this.)

Generally it would make sense to use linecache but we need to be careful because it's a bit thread unsafe.  It's usually not a problem for regular tracebacks in Python which is why you normally do not encounter this.  I  think we might have to test this under heavy load to see if it's an issue.

Small other note: linecache returns lines with newlines at the end, so we will have to strip them.

One more thing: I noticed that, at least in some cases, the call to `captureException()` inside `new_execute()` doesn't seem to be adding anything more than the usual call that occurs in the catch-all.

If you look in the base `Client` class [here](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/02aa7bee9addd1aaf2016af181c27a6ef9f37d01/raven/base.py#L177), you will see that `__init__()` already initializes `self.extra` with the value of `sys.argv` (at least when `context` is not `None`).

I have certainly seen in the logs identical values for both `argv` and `sys.argv` when an error occurs while running a management command.  So in a second sense, there is some duplication of information.

That again suggests to me that `new_execute()` shouldn't make an explicit call to `captureException()`.

```
    @property
    def _axes_plotting_order(self):
        axis_data = list(enumerate((self.nx, self.ny, self.nz)))
        sorted_data = sorted(axis_data, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
        return tuple(i for i, ax in sorted_data)
```

Sorting should be done on the axis number, not on the population of the bins.

Right now having nx=1, ny=10, nz=100 will lead to axis order Z,Y,X, while:
nx=1, ny=100, nz=10 will lead to order Y,Z,X.

> Q: How do I construct `InputPeerChannel` from a `Channel` if it can have no `access_hash`?
> A: That's the point. If you don't have the hash you shouldn't be able to access it for security reasons. The same applies to users, before `Channel`'s gained the `post_author` attribute there was a minimum version of the users without the `access_hash` if message signatures were enabled. This way you had a reference to get the user name to be shown as a signature, but you didn't have access to write directly to the author.

Ah okay, so the concept is that you have a big, powerful machine and want to do the real analysis later?
The reason I added the warning about Sessions is the following: The session takes much more space than the APK alone. Thus, if you have harddisk space considerations --> do not use sessions.
If you have TB of space, then you are good to go.
But I would not use sessions after all but just pickle the APK and Analysis object separately, as the DalvikVMFormats are stored in the Analysis Object as well.

Here is a quick demo how this could work:

```
from pickle import dump
from androguard.misc import AnalyzeAPK
from hashlib import sha512
import sys

a, _, dx = AnalyzeAPK('examples/tests/a2dp.Vol_137.apk')

sha = sha512()

sha.update(a.get_raw())

with open("{}_apk.p".format(sha.hexdigest()), "wb") as fp:
    dump(a, fp)

with open("{}_analysis.p".format(sha.hexdigest()), "wb") as fp:
    # It looks like here is the recursion problem...
    sys.setrecursionlimit(50000)
    dump(dx, fp)
```

Now, for the sizes:
```
31M	24a62690a770891a8f43d71e8f7beb24821d46a75e017ef4f4e6a04624105466621c96305d8e86f9900042e3ef1d5806a5d9ac873bebdf798483790446bd275e_analysis.p
852K	24a62690a770891a8f43d71e8f7beb24821d46a75e017ef4f4e6a04624105466621c96305d8e86f9900042e3ef1d5806a5d9ac873bebdf798483790446bd275e_apk.p
808K	examples/tests/a2dp.Vol_137.apk
```

That means, a 1M APK will explode into 30 times that easily!

I could bring down the size significantly, by using lzma:

```
from pickle import dump
from androguard.misc import AnalyzeAPK
from hashlib import sha512
import sys
import lzma

a, _, dx = AnalyzeAPK('examples/tests/a2dp.Vol_137.apk')

sha = sha512()

sha.update(a.get_raw())

with lzma.open("{}_apk.p.lzma".format(sha.hexdigest()), "wb") as fp:
    dump(a, fp)

with lzma.open("{}_analysis.p.lzma".format(sha.hexdigest()), "wb") as fp:
    # It looks like here is the recursion problem...
    sys.setrecursionlimit(50000)
    dump(dx, fp)
```

```
4,5M	24a62690a770891a8f43d71e8f7beb24821d46a75e017ef4f4e6a04624105466621c96305d8e86f9900042e3ef1d5806a5d9ac873bebdf798483790446bd275e_analysis.p.lzma
748K	24a62690a770891a8f43d71e8f7beb24821d46a75e017ef4f4e6a04624105466621c96305d8e86f9900042e3ef1d5806a5d9ac873bebdf798483790446bd275e_apk.p.lzma
```

The sizes could be further reduced if the bytecode would not be stored... But this would take some major refactoring in the code.

The correct location is NC_000011.9:g.32417913_32417917dup. Mutalyzer is incorrect in this case.

On NM_024426.4, the dup is correctly normalized in the 3' direction. The coordinates are initially projected to NC_000011.9:g.32417911_32417915dup. However, that variant is not properly normalized (3' shifted). The dup sequence (on the + strand) is ACCGT and the trailing sequence is ACAAG… . That means that the dup must be shifted right by the common prefix (AC), to result in NC_000011.9:g.32417913_32417917dup.

In other words, inserting ACCGT after 32417915 (NC_000011.9:g.32417911_32417915dup) has the same net effect as inserting CGTAC after 32417917 (NC_000011.9:g.32417913_32417917dup). The latter is correctly normalized.

I hadn't really considered a "thread-local" txaio loop (which obviously would just be "the" reactor for Twisted). I wonder: would this cover @soulmerge's use-cases?

Twisted I believe realizes the mistake of a global loop (and recent suggested-practice is to take a "reactor" argument for things that require it) -- but yes, it'll be a looong road to multiple reactors "actually" working in Twisted. I think the real mistake here is having an implicit reactor (which, unfortunately, asyncio also has).

Pragmatically, right now the only public API in Autobahn that accepts an extra `loop=` argument are the WebSocket factories -- and the merged #750 goes a decent way to at least making this "sort-of work when you squint" vs. txaio etc. (i.e. it fixes the original issue in this ticket). I don't believe anyone has ever asked for this from the WAMP factories.

As a refinement of "c" (basically @oberstet implies this mostly) we could do this: allow a `loop=` kwarg to `txaio.create_future()` (on all platforms). On Twisted, you get an exception `if loop is not reactor` or similar. On asyncio, one of two things could happen: either it's just blindly passed on to the `Future()` call using any global loop as a backup/default *or* it checks if there's a global `txaio` loop configured (and fails if you've configured a loop *and* passed in a loop). Then, we could selectively add ways to do custom loops for WebSocket (e.g. leave as now, probably) or WAMP protocols (would also take consideration for the `Connection` class -- which is probably where it make sense to do this for the WAMP side, instead of [or in addition to] `loop=` args on the WAMP factories).

I'd still like to see actual open-source asyncio code that does really use multiple loops ;)

(Personally, I'd recommend *strongly* against mixing threads and async code -- *especially* with WAMP, where you already have good tools to go multi-process which a) avoids confusion and b) gives way better performance). That said, asyncio does give you multiple event-loops out of the box (which is nice) and obviously people are using these even without threads.

One of the benefits of a `Command` application would actually be to make the difference between running and Exe and a command obvious and well defined. Basically:

1. `Executable` for an actual executable file you want to run. This would only accept an `IGangaFile` for the exe argument as it is specifically for running a file itself. A string would default to assuming a `LocalFile`

2. `Command` for running any generic command on the command line. Downside is you have to make sure the file is transferred correctly yourself, the upside is you can run anything you want.

I believe that the semantics we have for `Executable` at the moment were at least meant to be basically 1) above and so I think it makes things more obvious if we properly separate the two use cases rather than try to have the `Executable` app do too much.

The idea behind separating the Client and Server functionality is to only use/allocate resources when they are actually used.  For example, if a device only needs to implement Client functionality, it does not need to allocate resources needed to support Server functionality. @hubertmis That's great news. So let me summarize the outcome of this:
1. PR https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/pull/946 fixes that sometimes datagrams are unrightfully dropped when they contain a stale `ChangeCipherSpec`. That's what initially triggered this issue.
2. Patch https://github.com/mpg/mbedtls/commit/2694afbb56639f2135f1dd48b3ef903df6ac7ecd fixes that handshake messages are treated as fatal in `ssl_read` if `MBEDTLS_SSL_RENEGOTIATION` is disabled, a bug that makes the scenario in this issue continue to fail even after (1) has been fixed. This is actually a very good question that digs to an even more interesting topic of why there is the notion of a *project name* passed to `HookImplMarker` and `PluginManager` in the first place. Without noticing that difference there is no reason to choose one method over the other (calling the API from the client project with versus importing it from the host project).

It would probably be best if @hpk42 could comment as he is the original designer but I'll give my 2 cents after having maintained the code base a while.

With `pluggy.HookImplMarker("project")` and `pluggy.PluginManager("project")`, the `project_name` arg (here `"project"`) is a namespacing/association mechanism that is used for creating combined and adjacent *plugin spaces*. That is, it's a way to associate marks to managers using a key defined by the user such that multiple *plugin sets* can be defined and use together in the same system; the `PluginManager` is not supposed to be a singleton.

Most often the way you see it's usefulness is in projects that use `pluggy` whose dependencies **also use** `pluggy` resulting in `> 1` `pluggy.PluginManager` instances being used simultaneously. I don't think I've yet seen a project using `pluggy` that has more than one `PluginManager` internally although conceivably you could use it for some kind of internal plugin spec versioning (say you wanted to deliver some *next-gen* hook set alongside the current version that was opt-in for library consumers) or you could share implementation functions between multiple *plugin sets* / managers (again have yet to see this used in the wild) like some kind of *polymorphic plugins* spec.

To be honest I've always kind of thought that the `project_name` argument to the manager and marks was a bit strange because you are expecting the user to associate marks to managers *manually*. The same thing could have been accomplished by having each `PluginManger` generate marks for the user:
```python
from pluggin import PluginManager

ngpm = PluginManager()
legacy_hook = ngpm.mark('nextgen')
pm = PluginManager()
hook = pm.mark('legacy')
```
such that in your non-host project,
```python
from relies_on_pluggy import legacy_hook, hook

@legacy_hook
def myhook(arg):
    return do_legacy_stuff(arg)

@hook
def myhook(arg):
    return do_new_stuff(arg)
```
And (I think) this would mean the second approach (importing from the `host` project) would be that much more obvious and would expose the user to less `pluggy` internals as well as avoiding any potential name collisions (use `id(PluginManager)` as the value to each `HookimplMarker`).

@RonnyPfannschmidt @hpk42 @nicoddemus I'd be interested to hear your opinions on this.

To answer your questions:
>argues that the plugin writer doesn't have to care and should not have to do this and instead get the hookimpl marker directly from the host.

Either way the `HookImplMaker` instance referenced is a `pluggy` specific object; whether or not the `pluggy` API is called by the non-host project is really a matter of your own encapsulation preferences. The only down side is if the host project decides to change the `project_name` argument for some reason (lets say more managers/plugin-spaces are added for whatever reason) the consuming project's API call will have to change.

Because of this point that `pluggy` based projects may want to change their internal plugin namespacing I do think the **latter option** (importing the mark object directly) is probably the more maintainable approach.

>The second option both abstracts away "tox is using pluggy" and would enable tox to swap out plugin implementations (if need be) later on.

Well not unless the new impl works just like `pluggy` or do you mean *swap out `pluggy` implementations behind the scenes*? I would say it's not explicitly requiring the consuming project to `import` anything from `pluggy`, sure.

After few tests I discovered two pandas bugs that prevent you from running successfully alphalens.utils.get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns even when you correctly set quantile=1 or bins=1:

1 - if you set quantiles=1 then pandas.qcut raises `ValueError: Bin edges must be unique`. In my opinion 1 quantile and 1 value is fine and that shouldn't raise an exception and I believe this is a special case that the [code doesn't cover](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/master/pandas/tools/tile.py#L209).

2 - if you set bins=1 and quantiles=None it works most of the times except when the factor values is 0. In that case a `ValueError: Bin edges must be unique` is raised. This is a [bug too](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/master/pandas/tools/tile.py#L107) in my opinion.

Anyway, even if I'll submit a patch to pandas you won't see those fixes soon. So I was wondering if you really need to run alphalens on a single ticker (I don't actually see the usefulness) or if you were just letting us know about this issue.

Thanks.

I think it's pretty clear that submodels should only be instantiated if the process was initiated from a `Model` method or constructor.

Streamlink only searches in the `%PROGRAMFILES%`, `%PROGRAMFILES(X86)%`, and `%PROGRAMW6432%`. If you install VLC to a different drive (even if it's the same directory structure) then Streamlink won't be able to find it and you'll have to specify it using `--player`. The good news is you can set it in your `streamlinkrc` file so you don't have to specify it on the command line every time. See the docs: https://streamlink.github.io/cli.html#configuration-file, you'll just need to add 
```
player=S:\Program Files (x86)\VideoLAN\VLC\vlc.exe
```

Ah, it's a good thing you chimed in. I was thinking over why `encodeURI('a+b')` outputted `a+b` with no replacement, but it seems like `encodeURIComponent` is the way to go.

Regarding **problem 0** (We need to fetch sample counts per tag.): This is a hard problem to solve on the python side: The sample count may be unknown when the summary method is called (if the tensor is computed dynamically). Hence, we can't determine for instance how many tensor summaries to create and bundle into a group op. It also means we can't store the sample count within `ImagePluginData`.

`tf.summary.image` has the luxury of generating multiple tags in C++, when the sample count is known.
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f8363dc424f78ec06c9fe2faee7623624aa0392e/tensorflow/core/kernels/summary_image_op.cc#L153

If we are to somehow store the sample size within the summary tensor, we probably unfortunately have to make a new TensorFlow kernel for the tensor summary op. Or revisit whether we limit `tb.summary.image` to only support tensors of image data for which sample size is known when the op method is called. That might actually be OK (based on my gut call) for 95% of cases, but certain researchers who say dynamically generate some initially unknown X number of adversarial images may be hindered.

Regarding **problem 1** (We fetch individual images by index.), I also like the solution of refactoring frontend logic to fetch by step - and informing the user if the image at a step is no longer available. Many associated problems with that solution might go away if we end up choosing not to sample on disk.

The only solution I can think of is to adopt TensorFlow's `python_configure` rule. In order to do that, I want to fix this problem where it needs to be manually configured, i.e. the `Python Configuration Error: 'PYTHON_BIN_PATH' environment variable is not set` errors I thought I removed earlier.

I think there *might* be a way to make the summary conditionally write with TensorFlow ops, but that may be messy and unclear.

While I agree with your comment in #900 that it would be good to have a way to explicitly provide the frequencies (or probabilities) to the histogram/distribution dashboards, I'm not sure this specific approach is ideal because it seems like it conflates several different uses of histograms into the same summary method.

The issue I'm seeing is that adding `edges` to the existing method might suggest to users that they can explicitly list the `edges` of the buckets into which the values in `data` will be automatically binned (rather than the bucket edges being automatically determined based on `bucket_count` and the max and min of the data).  However, in this PR's implementation, `edges` is incompatible with the current auto-binning behavior because its presence causes `data` to be interpreted as the set of frequencies for each bucket instead of values to be binned.

We could largely resolve that by moving this functionality to a new summary method with a different name, but I still have a couple concerns about specifying `edges`:
- explicit `edges` isn't really well-supported by the current Histograms dashboard, which essentially re-bins everything (as far as I understand) across a linear set of x-values that are spaced across the min and max values of that histogram tag across all steps (not from any single histogram).  The result is that the original histogram bin edges in general don't make it forward to the final dashboard display, at least not if the min/max values change over time.  I'm curious to know whether you get a display that you actually find useful for your example in #900 where you only have 4 buckets, since I believe as implemented the histogram dashboard would re-distribute your frequencies across a whole bunch of smaller tick marks.
- explicit `edges` opens up the possibility of non-linear edges, i.e. buckets of different widths.  In that case it's misleading to plot the frequency as the height of the bucket; instead you want to plot the frequency density, i.e. divide out by the width of the bucket.  The frontend's re-distribution of frequencies might sort of accidentally accommodate this but it also might not (I haven't tried it) and either way it's not really doing it accurately, so to support non-linear edges we would likely have to rethink some of that logic as well.

We could sidestep those concerns by avoiding explicit `edges` and instead limiting the user to linearly spaced buckets (e.g. letting you specify min and max values which then autogenerate `bucket_count` linearly interpolated buckets), but I think we'd want to confirm that this is useful enough and weigh the trade off between doing that vs trying to add explicit edges support.

Lastly, I'm curious whether in #900 when you referred to categorical probabilities whether your categories have an inherent ordering or x-axis dimension to them, like restaurant star ratings (or put another way, do fractional values of your x-axis have any meaning?).  If that isn't the case, strictly speaking you want to plot a bar chart instead of a histogram (where ideally the x-axis is labeled by category rather than numbered), so the histogram plugin is a sub-optimal workaround for that kind of visualization anyway.

cc @dandelionmane @wchargin if you have any more context/insight here.

You can specify a custom condition clause, or in some simple cases, it will detect the relationship and automatically build the join condition.
```
In [9]: q = Query().from_table({'account': Account}).join({'user_table': User})
In [10]: q.get_sql()
Out[10]: 'SELECT account.* FROM account_account AS account JOIN auth_user AS user_table ON user_table.id = account.user_id'
```
```
In [11]: q = Query().from_table({'account': Account}).join({'user_table': User}, condition='account.user_id = user_table.id')
In [12]: q.get_sql()
Out[12]: 'SELECT account.* FROM account_account AS account JOIN auth_user AS user_table ON account.user_id = user_table.id'
```

`_txn_retry_loop` uses `with conn.begin():` (where `conn` is actually the session). This is supposed to be equivalent to the try/except block that calls commit or rollback. 

> Not closing is intentional as otherwise lazy loading will fail on objects if used generically for read and write.

Interesting. So if there's a lazy-loaded relationship, the objects created in the transaction are holding onto the Session for later lazy-loading? That seems like it might be an issue (but again, I never use the ORM half of SQLAlchemy so I might be misinterpreting). 


Sessions created by `run_transaction` have the `autocommit` flag set (because with this flag set, Sessions have an interface that is compatible with Connections). However, if such a session is accessed outside the transaction, it looks like it just [creates a new connection](https://github.com/zzzeek/sqlalchemy/blob/432d24ab1aac04a8ec881919964ff47ad8154659/lib/sqlalchemy/orm/session.py#L1042) without doing anything to arrange for it to be cleaned up. This seems like a bug in sqlalchemy, although this autocommit mode [is deprecated](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/session_transaction.html#session-autocommit). 

If this is the issue, then we can fix the leak by switching back to standard (non-autocommit) sessions and wrap them in an adapter object to give them the same interface as connections. However, any use of lazy loading runs the risk of getting a retryable error. I would recommend disabling lazy loading for any application using CockroachDB and always do your loading inside a transaction with `run_transaction`.

  • работать с методом главных компонент
  • использовать его для вычисления улучшенного индекса Доу-Джонса
    • ВведениеМетод главных компонент (principal component analysis, PCA) — это один из методов обучения без учителя, который позволяет сформировать новые признаки, являющиеся линейными комбинациями старых. При этом новые признаки строятся так, чтобы сохранить как можно больше дисперсии в данных. Иными словами, метод главных компонент понижает размерность данных оптимальным с точки зрения сохранения дисперсии способом.Основным параметром метода главных компонент является количество новых признаков. Как и в большинстве методов машинного обучения, нет четких рекомендаций по поводу выбора значения этого параметров. Один из подходов — выбирать минимальное число компонент, при котором объясняется не менее определенной доли дисперсии (это означает, что в выборке сохраняется данная доля от исходной дисперсии).В этом задании понадобится измерять схожесть двух наборов величин. Если имеется набор пар измерений (например, одна пара — предсказания двух классификаторов для одного и того же объекта), то охарактеризовать их зависимость друг от друга можно с помощью корреляции Пирсона. Она принимает значения от -1 до 1 и показывает, насколько данные величины линейно зависимы. Если корреляция равна -1 или 1, то величины линейно выражаются друг через друга. Если она равна нулю, то линейная зависимость между величинами отсутствует.ДанныеВ этом задании мы будем работать с данными о стоимостях акций 30 крупнейших компаний США. На основе этих данных можно оценить состояние экономики, например, с помощью индекса Доу-Джонса. Со временем состав компаний, по которым строится индекс меняется. Для набора данных был взят период с 23.09.2013 по 18.03.2015, в котором набор компаний был фиксирован (подробнее почитать о составе можно по ссылке из материалов). Одним из существенных недостатков индекса Доу-Джонса является способ его вычисления — при подсчёте индекса цены входящих в него акций складываются, а потом делятся на поправочный коэффициент. В результате, даже если одна компания заметно меньше по капитализации, чем другая, но стоимость одной её акции выше, то она сильнее влияет на индекс. Даже большое процентное изменение цены относительно дешёвой акции может быть нивелировано незначительным в процентном отношении изменением цены более дорогой акции.Реализация в sklearnМетод главных компонент реализован в пакете scikit-learn в модуле decomposition в классе PCA. Основным параметром является количество компонент (n_components). Для обученного преобразования этот класс позволяет вычислять различные характеристики. Например, поле explained_variance_ratio_ содержит процент дисперсии, который объясняет каждая компонента. Поле components_ содержит информацию о том, какой вклад вносят признаки в компоненты. Чтобы применить обученное преобразование к данным, можно воспользоваться методом transform. Для нахождения коэффициента корреляции Пирсона можно воспользоваться функцией corrcoef из пакета numpy.Материалы

Looked over the cheat sheet.  no idea what "standard" lexer means. My initial guess is that you have certain patterns that are very common, like does the lexer generate single tokens for keywords (like the SQL standard requires) i.e. some sequences of characters can only be used in rules in certain places.  If the sequence is a keyword, you can't use it in any other place.  SQL requires that if you use the word ORDER, it must never be used as a column name or a table name, as an example.  the letters can be used inside a quoted string, but not as a name.   

And of course, there is the opposite pattern, of saying the same string of letters may mean a variable name sometimes, and a type name sometimes, or sometimes a keyword. If you have a keyword tokenizer, it is a real pain to handle this pattern, as it complicates a lot of grammar rules. 

Another common keyword like pattern is when multiple punctuation characters in a particular order are considered to be separate tokens, like !=  or <>  or ** or && etc.  Getting the lexer to generate those tokens is really nice. 

Which brings up quoted strings. There are several characters used for quoted strings like (") or (') or (`) or (|) that begin and end particular types of strings. Systems seem to have only a few different ways to put the quoting character inside the string. Either doubling the start/end quote, or having a "escape" character that says the next character loses its special quoting quality like the backslash i.e. \"   or maybe just requiring a two different kinds of quotes, and using the other one to mark strings that have first quote character inside. 

You should be able to use certain quoting characters as stand alone tokens and not as begin and end quotes, as some languages use only some of them as quoting markers, and use the others as, for example, operators. This kind of recognizing can be stated in the rules, but the lexer shouldn't keep you from doing what you need to do.

Another common lexer option is to take a sequence of numeric characters and return it as a special token which has the number value of that sequence. 

Yet another issue is character pairs that must be balanced like ()  or {}  or <> or []  If you can find a way to recognize these kinds of patterns quickly, it will speed up grammar development (and error processing).

If you handle the most common kinds of patterns in the lexer, it makes life easier and quicker

I think the confusion here is the relationship between cmd2 and argparse. Python provides argparse and argparse has the ability to receive a static list of choices for arguments for the purpose of input validation. 
In cmd2 we have an autocompleter module that adds enhanced choice matching on top of argparse. Using setattr, we add additional information to arguments for the autocompleter to properly match dynamic completion choices with argparse arguments for the purposes of tab completion.
If you're using argparse for your commands, you should be able to pass the path_complete() function in to setattr().

As usual, I feel very silly after having found it.

Trick to tracking it down: for whatever reason, this time I was able to reproduce it by running `pytest` in a loop (specifically: `for X in $(seq 1000000); do echo $X; ~/pypy/pypy3.5-5.8-beta-linux_x86_64-portable/bin/pypy3.5 -u -m pytest -vs trio -k test_run_local_simultaneous_runs; done`, which would freeze after maybe 50 iterations). And then I added a bunch of print statements, and immediately went wtf, because the threads weren't executing in the order I expected at *all*, on *any* of the executions, including the ones that passed. (This is a test where we have threads but we manually control the scheduling in a very strict way... or so I thought.) And then the issue became obvious.

In the process I noticed that the test was written in a very fragile way, because we don't have a nursery abstraction for threads. Maybe it would be worth rewriting that test using a parent trio + two `run_sync_in_worker_thread` calls that each invoke their own trio, just for that? Probably the work needed to control the sequencing after that is too annoying to be worthwhile though (currently it involves both threads making blocking calls, which will also mess up the nice trio properties).

@robertnishihara, thanks very much for your suggestions!

I agree that we should use "tables" interface. That was the design actually, since the API does a bound of things together on Redis (add, publish, clean up). Sorry that I forgot to describe these details in the spec.

The idea of heartbeat is based on these thoughts:
    -- The machine may die, as you mentioned;
    -- The driver may hang or exit ungracefully, in that case node manager won't know the death.
The side impact of heartbeat is it will increase Redis load, but as node manager already use this way to find each other (heartbeat interval is 1 second), and we can actually adjust the interval used for drivers, I would think it is acceptable in general.
Cleaning up on node manager cannot work for machine failure case, which should be a valid concern in real production usage. Another side impact is doing in monitor process, we will have to create multiple redis connections during the process, but if we do in redis module, the overall performance should be better, right?

I have replied your comments in the spec, you can also take a look.

@SalahAdDin Sorry, havent had time to get back to this. We are using premailer internally on our project now but haven't time to get back to creating a stronger solution to be able to add it to oscar. 

The solution we are using does not use an external file as mentioned, trying to use an external file will cause issues.

Our email base.html template pulls in the css as a jinja template include like this
```
<style>
  {% include "customer/emails/partials/styles.css" %}
</style>
```

Now the CSS is inline in the head, then premailer can transform the html to inline all the styles as so. 
```
from premailer import transform
messages['html'] = transform(messages['html'])
```

New versions of `dill` are backward compatible with older versions, and support loading saved pickles from old versions of `dill`. 

For this particular case, there was a change made as the interface `dill.dill` should not be used, and thus was moved to `dill._dill` to highlight that it is private.  I argue that the solution here is to upgrade the older version of dill to `0.2.8.2`.

Is there a particular reason that you'd need a dependency on an older version of `dill`?  I realize that if you are using `dill` to ship objects between two systems, then yes, going from `0.2.8.2` to `0.2.7.1` won't work for objects that depend on the `dill._dill` reference until the older version is upgraded. 

If it's an absolute need.  The thing to do would be to find where `dill` stores the name of the module when it serializes the function, class, etc... and if it is `dill._dill` then store it as `dill.dill`.

PyInstaller is a program that freezes (packages) Python programs into stand-alone executables. https://www.pyinstaller.org/

> E.g. you cannot have two versions of Python systemwide

It depends on your system. The `python` executable will normally point to whatever you have installed - on some distributions it'll be linked to `python2.7`, other newer ones will link it to `python3.x` (whatever they've selected) - but you can have multiple versions installed.

> you need to have python, pip, and virtualenv installed (system-wide), and then you can do:

Right. See all 4 of those steps are encapsulated into just one command when you use `tox`.

When you invoke `tox`, tox will:

* Automatically create the virtualenvs
* Install the dependencies (that's what `bootstrap` does correct?)
* Run all the tests

I think that the first thing you want to do is to adjust the parameters of the camera.

If you're only interested in capturing the plant, then you should adjust the **Depth Clamp Min/Max** under _Stereo Module/Advanced Controls/Depth Table_ in the Intel RealSense Viewer. You'll capture only a small part of your scene, plus that may help you "get rid" of the light in the ceiling (which is never helping btw).
I don't know if it's going to work for you, but I suggest you then set the **Depth Units** to 100 (under _Advanced Controls/Depth Table_), and the other **Depth Units** to 0.0001 (under _Controls_).
That being done, try adjusting the **Disparity Shift** (under _Advanced Controls/Depth Table_) and maybe the **Exposure** and the **Laser Power** (under _Controls_) depending on the lighting conditions. 
Finally, enable the **Post-Processing** for the max achievable quality. (Obviously, there is a lot more parameters, but these ones are a good start).
Just export the ".json" if you don't want to use the Viewer in your workflow next time.

One thing being weird though, is that you seem to lose some data when you export the ".ply", I don't know if it's going to correct that.

Just putting a note here that loompy 2 changes the behaviour of `loompy.create`: it no onger returns a value. Thus in order to work with a newly created file you have to do this:

```python

loompy.create("filename.loom", m, row_attrs, col_attrs)
with loompy.connect("filename.loom") as ds:
    ....do something with ds
# File closes automatically
```

The reason for the change is that I found myself often creating files without closing the returned file handle, which causes issues especially in multi-process scenarios.

Hello @scanny ,

Thank you very much for the explanation in detail, it really helps :). I understand more now.

I am totally agree with you that we should escape special chars in `Hyperlink.address`. ^^

For \<w:history\>, I think you are right. In this [document of Microsoft](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/documentformat.openxml.wordprocessing.hyperlink(v=office.14).aspx), it says
> The history attribute value of true specifies that the target of the current hyperlink must be added to a list of visited hyperlinks when invoked within the document. end example]
The possible values for this attribute are defined by the ST_OnOff simple type (§22.9.2.7).

So the value can be 0/1/True/False. 
But I don't think this \<w:history\> is important as \<w:history\> has not been set by google docx nor by open office. I suggest we don't implement  \<w:history\> at the moment.

As you have mentioned, \<w:anchor\> is a bookmark name with '#' prepended, either for the document itself (no \<r:id\>) or for an external uri (with  \<r:id\>). If neither \<r:id\> and the bookmark is present, the \<w:anchor\> is just a harmless bookmark name, e.g., as a link '#anchor_value'.
_PS_. [document of Microsoft](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/documentformat.openxml.wordprocessing.hyperlink(v=office.14).aspx) suggests that if the hyperlink is an external uri, the anchor attribute should be ignored. But in practice, Word put the anchor to the end of the uri with '#' prepended. I find this behavior is more reasonable as it corresponds to the behavior of the html anchors.
Therefore,  we might want to keep the \<w:anchor\> of the hyperlink, even `Hyperlink.address` is `None` and the bookmark cannot find in the document itself. How do you think?
