I try to explain here:
A project should always declare all of its dependencies in the setup.py either in the install_requires, setup_requires or extras. All derived functions like tox.ini should just use this settings, and explicite declare what it also needs to run. Even if that is redundant, like isort.
All packages should be usable, developable and testable without external systems. If I can give my 2 cents, I have the feeling that the GPL choice (as Tim Smith also expressed) was the right choice a few years ago, when the culture around open source and sharing was really different, when the web was much less invaded by web projects/frameworks and when big companies feared open source.
In my humble opinion, nowadays, the culture around open source projects has changed: with this "rush" to release software as open source, that hit a very large part of companies that are working with the web, it becomes natural, the way to do, to people to contribute back, and not the contrary. If it was not the case, GPL would have been chosen by most of the projects, wouldn't it? https://www.whitesourcesoftware.com/whitesource-blog/open-source-software-licenses-trends/

A very similar topic is discussed [here](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=3870700), when Meteor js long time ago (5 years ago!!!) decided to change license from GPL to MIT, and in the infinite discussion on HackerNews, a couple of comments can, in my opinion, summarize and maybe help to understand the choice of CERN/Invenio:
```
GPL restricts the pool of potential collaborators.

Also, GPL has served its purpose: these days 1) almost everyone realizes that it is in
everyone's interest to share code and contribute back (maintaining private forks is
expensive in the long term), and 2) most software is offered as a service and the value
is not just in the software, but also in the infrastructure, maintenance, support, etc,
meaning you rarely encounter the problem of restricted liberties because of a
software license.
```

```
GPL requires you to give back; MIT gives you the choice. Developers are by nature creative
people. And humans are not incapable of altruism. Even with an MIT license, I expect
a lot of community contributions to pour in.

In fact, I expect more community contributions to come in. MIT means (perhaps) a lower
percentage of users will give back, but there will be a higher absolute number of users
since the license is more permissive.
```

I think what happened is that your original interpolation code used the parent ChromaticObject.evaluateAtWavelength() method which doesn't actually reference any of the stored monochromatic images, but just creates the appropriate GSObject on-the-fly.  In @rmjarvis 's refactorization, which implements interpolation in the InterpolatedChromaticObject subclass, the InterpolatedChromaticObject.evaluateAtWavelength() method returns an InterpolatedImage formed from the stored monochromatic images.  So in the new code there's some interpolation going on, with potentially mismatched stepk, maxk, and so on.  I'd guess that 4 decimal places is reasonable in this case.

@jmeyers314 - I wanted to run some things by you, to make sure I understand the SED/bandpass functionality properly.  

I believe I now understand why the WFIRST bandpass AB zeropoints were too faint in v1.4 and too bright in v1.5:

- Rubab is (I'm pretty sure) expecting the zeropoint for WFIRST bandpasses to be set for an object producing 1 count/sec as seen by WFIRST.

- In v1.4, the zeropoint was set for an object producing 1 count per 168 sec exposure as seen by WFIRST, so there was an extra +2.5*log10(168) compared to Rubab's expected convention, which gave something too faint.

- In v1.5, the new convention sets the zeropoint for an object producing 1 count/sec/cm^2, so we were missing that area factor (i.e., that WFIRST has a collecting area way larger than 1 cm^2) and the zeropoint was too bright.

First of all, does my logic here make sense? I confirmed that if I include that area factor in the WFIRST bandpass zeropoints (see branch for this issue), the zeropoints are now ~27.  So in principle, I could just wrap this up by adding some unit tests, but I have a few questions about this:

1. Do you know what the typical convention is for zeropoint definition?  For example, for ACS the norm is to use the "1 photon/sec" after including the factor of collecting area.  What I'm proposing to do for WFIRST is consistent with that.  Should we be including some examples in demos or some documentation to clarify that if people have a particular instrument in mind they must be careful to include that collecting area factor?  Should we perhaps include it as an option in the `withZeropoint` method rather than as a correction afterwards, as I've currently done?

2. Sorry if I missed something, but I want to make sure we self-consistently demonstrate or explain usage of SEDs with zeropoints and how to properly set the flux given some exposure time somewhere.  Where is this currently done?  Currently because of the way the flux is set in demo13.py, we sidestep this issue there.

@yliaog this is an issue for us, as we are running kubernetes pods as batch processes and tracking the state of the pods with a static client. Once the client object is initialized, it does no refresh, and therefore any job that takes longer than 60 minutes will fail. Looking through python-base, it seems like we could make a wrapper class that generates a new client (or refreshes the config) every `n` minutes, or checks status prior to every call (as @mvle suggested). The best fix would be in swagger-codegen, but a temporary solution would probably be very useful for a lot of people.

@jmwoloso Two issues:

- Your code is blowing up in the call to `bq_client.create_table` with a `409 Confict` error, because the table already exists.  Unlike with jobs which implicitly create tables, there is no `WRITE_DISPOSITION` configuration for the call.  Just wrap the call in  a `try: ... except: pass` block, e.g.:

```python
from google.api_core import exceptions
...
    try:
        table = bq_client.create_table(table)
    except exceptions.Conflict:
        pass
```

Further down, you configure the query job with an invalid string constant for the write disposition.  The actual literal is `'WRITE_TRUNCATE'`, but you would be better off using the enumerated constant, e.g.:

```python
job_config.write_disposition = bq.WriteDisposition.WRITE_TRUNCATE
```

Similarly for the priority:
job_config.priority = bq.QueryPriority.INTERACTIVE
